chapters.summary: Committee staff calls the joint hearing to order, reminding attendees to silence electronic devices and outlining the procedures for witness testimony.
chapters.title: Committee Staff Instructions and Hearing Call to Order

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, and welcome to today's New York City Council hearing from the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure joint with the Committee on Consumer and Worker Protections.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this point, going forward, I'd like to remind everyone to please silence their electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And at no point going forward, is anyone to approach the Dais unless invited to testify?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you'd like to sign up to testify, and you have not, please fill out a witness testimony slip at the table in the back of the room with the Sergeant Arms.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, we're ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin welcomes attendees and provides historical context on Local Law 121 of 2023, which established the permanent 'Dining Out NYC' program to transition from temporary pandemic-era rules.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Opening Remarks: Background on Local Law 121 and the Permanent Outdoor Dining Program

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so in August 2023, the council passed Local Law 121, which established a permanent outdoor dining program by granting licenses and revocable consents to restaurants to operate sidewalk and roadway cafes across all five boroughs of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This legislation learned the lessons of the city's temporary open restaurants program, improved upon the city's old sidewalk cafe law, and expanded the use of sidewalks and roadways to restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: By the end of the city's temporary open restaurants programs, there were 13,144 businesses participating in outdoor dining.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin expresses her long-standing concerns regarding the Department of Transportation's ability to manage a licensing program, highlighting severe delays that have left small businesses in uncertainty.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Opening Remarks: Concerns Over DOT's Management and Application Backlogs

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: An agency that does not have background in this area.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Unfortunately, these concerns were not addressed, and DOT was given management of this program.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Despite these concerns, Local Law 121 allowed restaurants that submitted a dining-out application to DOT by the August 3, 2024 deadline, to continue operating the application review during the application review process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: However, eight months after the application deadline, DOT struggles to review and move remote revocable consent applications through the process in a timely manner, basically leaving restaurants in a state of absolute uncertainty about the nature of their outdoor dining setups in peak season.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I cannot tell you the number of restaurants across the city that have contacted my office so incredibly frustrated about these serious delays.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So here we are on April 22nd, 2025, which is the beginning of the peak season of outdoor dining, and we should be having a robust outdoor dining program, which is what the council had intended, that provides a lifeline to our small businesses and also activates the streets.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin highlights that only 67 restaurants have completed the full application process, representing less than 2% of total applicants, and calls the rollout disastrous for small businesses.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Opening Remarks: Low Approval Rates and Restaurant Uncertainty

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: However, just 67 restaurants, I repeat, just 67 restaurants, and this is coming from DOT's own data, have completed their entire outdoor dining application process and received a revocable consent to operate a sidewalk cafe or a roadway cafe.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This represents less than 2% of the approximately 3,800 dining out NYC applications from more than 3,000 restaurants that DOT received.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: DOT started to address this problem only weeks before the roadway dining season was set to begin on April 1st, 2025 by issuing conditional approvals to a number of roadway dining setups.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As of yesterday, DOT provided 726 conditional approvals to restaurants to operate a roadway cafe, which would only apply to those restaurants that have made it through DOT's review process and the community board review process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I'll get to later during the testimony the concerns that we are now giving community boards major backlogs because of this large delay, and that also is not equitable or fair or sensible.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: DOT also authorized the continued operation of 1,808 sidewalk cafes while their applications are pending with the department.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So now we have a situation where hundreds upon hundreds of restaurants are awaiting approval and frankly do not know whether they should make the necessary investments in their outdoor setups for the current season.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This rollout, in short, has been nothing short of disastrous.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The permanent and long-awaited outdoor dining season is upon us, and DOT is woefully unprepared to meet this moment for thousands of small businesses and their eager patrons.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We look forward today to hearing from DOT about the issues that are stalling this process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We would like to learn how we can improve application processing times and the user friendliness of the application process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We also look forward to hearing firsthand accounts from restaurant owners, and I want to thank all the restaurant owners and advocacy groups and community board leaders who have come out today to testify.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We absolutely have to address this and improve this process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I'm now going to turn it over to my colleague, Chair Brooks Powers, for her opening remarks.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and good morning, and thank you all for joining today's joint hearing of the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure and the Committee on Consumer and Worker Protection.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers introduces the joint hearing focus on the Dining Out NYC program and proposed Intro 857 regarding the towing of vehicles that encumber city streets.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Opening Remarks: Introduction of Intro 857 and Joint Hearing Focus

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: My name is Sylvina Brooks Powers, and I am the chair of the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I am pleased to be joined today by my colleague Chair Menon.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Today's hearing will focus on the dining out NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In addition, we will be hearing proposed intro number 857A, sponsored by Councilmember Salamanca, in relation to towing vehicles that are an encumbrance on the street.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The Department of Transportation is tasked with regulating and maintaining the city's sidewalks and streets.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers outlines the evolution of outdoor dining from pre-pandemic sidewalk cafes to the temporary pandemic-era program and the subsequent enactment of Local Law 121.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Opening Remarks: Evolution of Outdoor Dining and Local Law 121

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As my co-chair discussed prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, sidewalk cafes were regulated by the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection and could only be located in certain districts.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In response to the pandemic, the council enacted Local Law 54 of 2020, which required the city to waive and refund all revocable consent fees for unenclosed sidewalk cafes due between March 1st, 2020 and February 28, 2021.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In June of 2020, the city established a temporary open restaurants program to expand outdoor seating options for food service establishments citywide.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And two separate programs managed by DOT enabled temporary outdoor dining, open restaurants and open streets full closure.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: By the end of the open restaurants program, there were 13,144 participating businesses.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In addition, 361 existed on open streets.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In August of 2023, the council enacted local law 121 of 2023, local law 121, which repealed the pre-pandemic sidewalk cafe program and established a new permanent outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: With this legislation, the council applied lessons learned from the temporary open restaurants program to improve upon the old sidewalk cafe law.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: While maintaining aspects of the program meant to ensure outdoor dining is done in a safe and clean manner, with deference to residents and neighborhood needs throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: While the old pre-pandemic outdoor dining program was managed by DCWP, the permanent outdoor dining program established by Local Law 121 is managed by DOT.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Under this program, there are no longer zoning restrictions on the locations of sidewalk or roadway cafes.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In addition, the law increased the license term from two to four years and lowered license fees and revocable consent fees.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: However, in the months leading up to the launch of open dining in April 2025, concerns were raised by businesses, advocates, and local officials about the program's implementation.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers details the implementation challenges reported by businesses, including onerous online forms, lack of language accessibility, high construction and storage costs, and changed clearance standards.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Opening Remarks: Implementation Challenges, Costs, and Accessibility

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: DOT has struggled to process applications in a timely manner.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As of April 10th, 2025, just 60 restaurants, less than 2% of the applications received by DOT completed the entire outdoor dining application process and received revocable consent to operate a sidewalk or roadway cafe, which equates to less than 2% of the 3,400 applications received by DOT.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: According to the New York Times, hundreds of applications were deemed incomplete by DOT.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The most common issue being restaurants' failure to provide adequate information on their site plans.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Although conditional approvals have been granted, this process is still not where it should be.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Businesses have also reported issues with the application process, including having to fill out onerous online forms and a lack of language accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We have also received complaints about additional costs associated with the program's setup regulations and the required seasonality of the program.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: This has impacted the participation of some restaurants because the approved setups are costly, and the associated construction and storage costs make it too expensive and cumbersome to be viable for some businesses.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Finally, many of the rules promulgated by DOT for the locations of sidewalk cafes differ from the pre-pandemic standards.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: This has left out some restaurants that previously participated in the outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: At today's hearing, I want to find out what DOT is doing to ensure that the dining out program is effective, safe, and equitable.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In addition, I want to look at how we can make the application process easier for business owners to navigate and to improve upon implementation.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Before we begin, I would like to thank my staff and committee staff for their hard work.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: John Basile, senior policy analyst, Kevin Kotowski, Senior Policy Analyst, Mark Chen, Senior Counsel to the Committee, Adrian Drippal, Senior Financial Analyst, Julian Martin, my policy and budget director, and Renee Taylor, my chief of staff.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I will now ask the committee council to swear in the administration.

chapters.summary: Chair Brooks-Powers transitions the hearing to receive testimony from public advocates and restaurant owners first, in order to address their concerns before hearing from the administration.
chapters.title: Transition to Public Advocate and Business Panel

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We're actually gonna go with um advocates first.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We're gonna hear from advocates groups, and the reason why is we want to make sure that we are getting the concerns addressed first before we hear from the administration.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So before I call the panel, I want to recognize my colleagues that have joined Councilmember Banks, Councilmember Osay, Councilmember Brewer, and we'll call additional colleagues as they come in.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So our first panel today is Andrew Ridgie from the New York Hospitality Alliance, Robert Bookman from the New York Hospitality Alliance, Max Bookman from the New York Hospital Hospitality Alliance, Chrisette Woods from Sylvia's Restaurant, Megan Rickardson from Someday Bar, Robert Gorino from Nizza.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: If you could all please come forward.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And please begin.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: My name is Andrew Ridgeie, and I am the executive director of the New York City Hospitality Alliance.

chapters.summary: Andrew Rigie, Executive Director of the NYC Hospitality Alliance, testifies on the dramatic decline in outdoor dining participation, the failure of citywide equity goals, and outlines seven proposed reforms.
chapters.title: Andrew Rigie Testimony: NYC Hospitality Alliance

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We are a not-for-profit association that represents restaurants and bars across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And I'd like to thank you for uh having this hearing today and having us testify.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So Dining Out NYC promised to make New York City's Alfresco dining the envy of the world, turning streetscapes into vibrant public spaces, supporting small businesses, protecting jobs, and building the nation's premier outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: But that promise is slipping.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Just 3,000 restaurants have applied, according to the Department of Transportation's press release from February.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Far fewer than the 13,000 that participated during the pandemic, or even the six to eight thousand estimated to still be operating in the summer of 2024.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Many of these small businesses face steep regulatory and financial hurdles.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: This summer we may see little more outdoor dining than we did before the uh pandemic when there are approximately 1,200 licensed sidewalk cafes, and they will still mostly be located south of 90 Strict 6th Street in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: That's failing the new program's goal of citywide equity, which was a hallmark of the pandemic era program and which lawmakers sought to replicate.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So, before the pandemic, for example, the Bronx had just 26 sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: During the pandemic, that number surged to about 1,010 outdoor dining setups, including both sidewalk and roadway cafes.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Now, under the new Dining Out NYC program, that figure has dropped dramatically to around 78.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: According to DOT data, there are currently only 2,491 restaurants with outdoor dining citywide.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Compared to the previous pandemic numbers, that's 7,300 fewer roadway cafes, 9,600 fewer sidewalk cafes, and about 10,300 fewer restaurants offering outdoor dining overall.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Even with 100 or 1,000 applications still in the pipeline, no matter how you slice and dice the data, it's clear the outdoor dining revolution we are hoping for is not here.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: But now's not the time to point blame and fingers.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Today is the time for the mayor and the city council to join us at let's call it the outdoor dining table and fulfill the bold vision of dining out NYC.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I'm going to briefly outline seven reforms we're proposing to help achieve this goal, and then our council Rob Bookman, who has decades of experience with outdoor dining, we'll get into some more of the specifics, and you'll hear from local restaurateurs about the different challenges that they've faced and their experience with outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Number one, year-round outdoor dining in the roadway.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Making the program seasonal was a big problem.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We said this from the beginning, we knew it would happen.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: It's too expensive to set up outdoor dining, then take it down, store it during the winter months, only to set it up again as cost prohibitive for small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We need to reduce fees across the board.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I know there's a lot of concerns in specific neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I see council member Brewer, who I recall in these chambers, voiced her concern about why in her neighborhood it costs more than in every other neighborhood for the most part.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Uh, but I think uh Chair Menon, you uh have some of the highest fees as well in your district as well for restaurants to participate.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Sidewalk cafe enclosures where restaurants are paying for a year-round license but unable to use it because they can't winterize it with an enclosure, has been a major problem.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Clearances, you'll hear from restaurateurs about reduced clearances compared to what they used to have, has reduced their outdoor dining and posed big problems.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: The application process, as the chair expressed, uh, has had many challenges.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Scaffolding, which we're always talking about, getting it down.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I think it was from Manhattan to Montreal.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: If you lined up all the scaffolding, I know uh uh uh Commissioner Otto's working hard to get it down.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: The council passed some bills, the mayor signed, but there's still issues related to scaffolding and outdoor dining, and the processing time, as was expressed, is taking way too long.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Now, notwithstanding that, I do want to give a shout out to some of the people, particularly Penny Ringel over at DOT, who's been working really hard and is very responsive.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Um, so while there certainly are challenges, we also want to recognize people that are trying to make the best out of a challenging situation.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And that's why we need reform.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And in closing, what I will just say is that New York City still has the potential to build a world class outdoor dining program, but only if the city leaders collectively come together and fix the reform and propose and enact reforms.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Without this, we're going to risk squandering a once in a generation opportunity to build the best outdoor dining program that benefits restaurants, workers, communities they serve, and create a lot of economic activity for the city and reach the promise of not only having the greatest restaurants anywhere in the world, but the greatest outdoor dining anywhere in the world.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: My name is Rob Bookman.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I'm a counsel to the New York City Hospitality Alliance, one of its founding members.

chapters.summary: Robert Bookman, Counsel to the NYC Hospitality Alliance, discusses structural issues with the revocable consent process, stricter clearance rules, and the financial hurdles preventing small restaurants from applying.
chapters.title: Robert Bookman Testimony: NYC Hospitality Alliance

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Also have been a partner in a boutique law firm called Pazetski and Bookman.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We specialize in licensing and regulatory matters.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: But I've been involved with every aspect of the changing rules and regulations and the laws on sidewalk cafes for 40 years from both inside government and on the other side of government.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And we were deeply involved with two mayoral administrations and the city council in negotiating both this new statute and the rules with the Department of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: As with any once in a generational piece of legislation, there were many, many compromises that were made.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Some we supported, some we did not.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: For the most part, we hoped for the best, and we believed that the council and administration did as well.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We understood that we would give these series of compromises, all of us, our best chance of success.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We would see what resulted, and we would come back.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And here we are.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Unfortunately, we now know that no one who supports outdoor dining can say that this program has been a success.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I say that without any malice, without pointing any fingers at anyone.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There are no bad guys here, there are no enemies.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The program simply has not worked out as well as we all had hoped for.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There were mistakes in the legislation that were part of compromises that DOT had to live with.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There were mistakes in their rulemaking that were part of compromises that the industries had to live with.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So there are a series of issues which have brought us where we are today.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I also want to point out specifically Pinkus Ringel, who is not here unfortunately, nor the commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I'm surprised neither one are here.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Both of them have been extremely cooperative working with us.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Ringle is uh one of the best public servants I've ever seen, quite frankly.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: He responds to emails on weekends in the middle of the night.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They're very responsive.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: But nevertheless, we have a structure that is not successful.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We need to recognize that it's not a success, despite everyone's best interest efforts, and look at the reasons why to and come up with solutions together.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The shockingly no number of restaurants that even bothered to apply for outdoor dining, let alone hundreds of those who applied but are not following through on their applications, and Max will talk a little bit about that, makes it clear that the program needs to be fixed.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Obviously, we all knew with the emergency over and the program no longer free that there would be a drop-off in the number of restaurants participating.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's clear.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I'm a straight shooter, I have a reputation testifying here for 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Uh, you know, I'm not gonna blow smoke, I'll tell you the truth and I'll answer your questions truthfully.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: But frankly, we thought there would be a lot more than the 3,000 establishments that applied.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We thought about double that number.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So the alliance asked our membership why so many did not apply, and the answers were clear in our survey.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The new stricter clearance requirements, as compared to the old law that nobody anticipated, which came out in the rules, made it more difficult for people to get sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The overall expense in relationship to those lower number of tables made some people decide it wasn't worth it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The seasonal nature of roadway has really made it many people decide that it's not worth it, and that program has been decimated based on the number of applications that are filed.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And again, you will hear that there are potentially hundreds of people who did a file who are not following through now that they see how expensive it is to set it up.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The difficulty of many small businesses applying online added to the confusion and the expense that restaurants simply could not afford.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The consent, one of the compromises that was in the legislation was that sidewalk cafes would continue with the consent process in addition to a license.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Let me explain that for a minute.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There were two major structural problems that were brought into sidewalk cafes in the 1980s that caused so few licenses, and 70% centered in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And that was the zoning laws, which restricted it in out of boroughs, and the consent.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The consent makes it a long process, a complicated process, an expensive process.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: It adds rent above a license fee.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We wanted to get rid of both.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Only zoning was eliminated.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Same thing like when we got rid of uh the Cabaret licensing law.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We said that's not going to make dancing legal because the zoning law is what restricts dancing.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And finally, years later, zoning was addressed.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So we had both of those problems here.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Many commercial users of the public sidewalks don't have both of those.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They just have a license.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Thousands of street vendors just have a license.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Fruit and vegetable stands, which are very much like sidewalk cafes, movable structures, they just have a license.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They just have a license.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: None of them have this consent on top of it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: By keeping the consent on top of it, we created an expensive, complicated problem for the thousands of people that only experience outdoor dining during during the emergency, which was a simple go online, say yes to a few questions, and you got your permit.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Requiring the act.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So look let me give a few examples though, in some of the what Andrew talked about.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Processing time.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: As a matter of policy.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The statute says that DOT must within five days of receiving an application determine if it is complete, and if it is complete, they're supposed to start the time clock by sending it to the community board and the other agencies that review sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Same language that existed when it was with the consumer affairs for 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: DOT unfortunately interpreted that to mean not just that the application is complete, meaning all the documents are there, the photographs, the filing fee, but that it's an approvable application, meaning their entire review has to be done before they started the clock.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Well, that's a multi-month process, as it should be.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They have experts, examiners who review the plans, look at the pictures, get back to the applicant, which Max will talk about.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They're very responsive.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We respond to those, make some changes, uh, listen to the community board.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: When once the application was deemed complete and approved by them, then they first started the clock.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And that's why we're in a situation where this late in the process from August 3rd, when the applications were filed, where we just don't have many approved.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Another couple of quick ones.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The online only, which has been discussed, has hurt a lot of small restaurants, immigrant restaurants, English is not their first language.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They couldn't know how they don't know how to upload and download, and they couldn't save their work.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um, and now what we're seeing is a third rookie mistake is they're requiring, unlike consumer affairs, the entire annual rent up front.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Nobody pays an annual rent up front.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: If you had to do that at home or in your business, you wouldn't have a business.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Consumer affairs always allowed quarterly payments.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There's nothing in the statute which prohibits that.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So the variety of issues that you know need to be addressed.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Uh, we can address it, but we all have to first come to the conclusion that what we have is not working, that we can correct these problems.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They're all correctable.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Some changes will can be accomplished with a simple policy shift, some with rule changes, some with statutory changes.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We all have a role here.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: But this is a program that can be corrected.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Finally, the whole process here was to create a balance between the old law, which was way too restrictive, and the emergency law, which was way too permissive because it was an emergency.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We were trying to come to a balance.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We now see, unfortunately, we did not reach the correct balance, and we need to readdress these issues so we can come to a middle ground.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Many changes were positive.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: No outdoor music, no outdoor TVs, the flooring on roadways have to be movable and cleaning, hours of operation that are restricted.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We addressed many of the problems, but we did not address what we see here, which is why businesses aren't applying.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And we thank you for the opportunity to set the table here.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And you'll briefly hear from Max and some of the restaurants that are having these issues.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Thank you, members.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: My name is Max Bookman.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: I'm a seventh-generation New York City resident chairman, and for the last 10 years, I've lived in your district.

chapters.summary: Max Bookman, Partner at a licensing law firm, shares his experience managing over 200 DOT outdoor dining applications and describes the process as overly confusing and frustrating for business owners.
chapters.title: Max Bookman Testimony: NYC Hospitality Alliance

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: My wife and I are raising our two kids there.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: My daughter goes to PS 158, and we are avid, enthusiastic outdoor dining participants.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: I'm one of the several million people in New York City who enthusiastically support the notion of outdoor dining and want to see it succeed.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: When I'm not at home, I'm a partner at a law firm, and for the last 10 years of my legal career, I've represented bars, restaurants, and other businesses in various licensing matters, including before the Department of Consumer Affairs, before they changed the name, uh, as well as now the Department of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Um, before you hear from the voices of some of the restaurant owners who've come to testify before you today, I just wanted to share with you uh a common theme that we we couldn't bring all, you know, hundreds of bars and restaurant owners who participate in this program and are frustrated.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: And my firm alone, I'm responsible for managing over 200 DOT outdoor dining applications.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: So I'm there in the trenches.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: If any of the members uh have any questions that are detail-oriented about what it's like in the trenches of the DOT application process, I'm happy to answer them.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: But one common theme that we hear, you'll hear today, but we hear from bar and restaurant owners throughout this process is that it's just too confusing.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: It's just too frustrating and too confusing.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: The various steps are too confusing, the the information that they get from the agency is too confusing.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Um, how it interacts with the state liquor authority is too confusing.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Um I have a law degree, it's my job to know this stuff.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: It's confusing for me to be able to explain this process to my clients in in ways that they understand.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Bar and restaurant owners, it's not their job to have to understand how this process works.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: It's their job to run a bar or a restaurant and be successful business people, employ staff and make a living for them and to contribute to their community.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Um, everyone has had to become experts in this process in a way that they really shouldn't have to.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Um, so to echo some of the prior comments that you've heard.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Let's all roll up our sleeves, work together, not point fingers, acknowledge that there's a problem, and see what we could do to fix the problem.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: My name is Chrisette Woods, and I am the second generation owner of Sylvia's restaurant, which has been serving the Harlem community since 1962.

chapters.summary: Chris Woods, second-generation owner of Sylvia's Restaurant in Harlem, testifies about the confusion in the application process and the need for winterized setups to maintain employment and seating capacity.
chapters.title: Chris Woods Testimony: Sylvia's Restaurant

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: And I do remember us when we got our first sidewalk license over maybe close to 30 years, over 20 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: But I want to say that I want to thank the hospitality alliance for helping us as much as they can to navigate through these the changes through this application process, which has been, as they say repeatedly, it has been extremely confusing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: Communication has been good, I would say, but it has been the challenge of not being able to plan, you know, waiting for the approval, not being able to plan as we are in the season.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: The other thing I would like to express is having something enclosed during the winter months will help us to continue the hours, continue to provide additional seating.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: Our outdoor space can employ up to two to three people per shift per um five to seven days a week.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chrisette Woods
utterances.text: So having that throughout the winter months will be very helpful to an already challenging operation and business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Megan Rickerson.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I own um Sumday Bar in Borham Hill, Brooklyn.

chapters.summary: Megan Rickerson, owner of Someday Bar in Boerum Hill, provides a detailed financial breakdown of the high costs of compliance, explaining that the seasonal roadway rules make the program financially unviable for neighborhood spots.
chapters.title: Megan Rickerson Testimony: Someday Bar

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I also serve on the Hospitality Alliance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I'm on the Mayor's Small Business Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I serve on my bid and I do a lot.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And I open my bar right before COVID, my very first business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I am an expert at the emergency program because I rebuilt my structure so many times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I have photos sawing plywood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I am not a carpenter, but I learned very quickly that I had to, you know, as we say pivot, we had to say pivot a lot.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: But we made it and I'm here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And I am now entrenched in advocacy because I've realized how important it is to have connections with all of you in order to survive in a city like this, with so many regulations and so many people walking in through our doors, you know, checking in on us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I decided to go headfirst into the program, and I'm not gonna lie, I feel a little duped because it was presented to me in a way that this was supposed to be supportive, and it was supposed to be easier than what existed before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And I can tell you that it's not, I didn't ever do the first program, but what is presented is not easy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Um, and it's not affordable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I have a neighborhood bar, we are not $20 cocktails, we are not $45 steaks, we are $19 burgers, we are $6 happy hour drinks, we are $3 hot dogs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And based on the money that I have put in so far, which I have numbers just to really like break it down because it's easy to say something is expensive and not know the numbers behind it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: For my sidewalk, um, my check to DOT and the comptroller's office was set $1,775 for six seats.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: For my street, $4,573 for 16 seats, all right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: The application was so confusing that I did pay a lawyer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Um I had to pay per application.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I also had to pay to have my hatch certified, and I had to pay an architect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And as it was stated by Miss Menon that a lot of people haven't completed their DOT applications because they find that part very difficult, I hired an architect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And I understand that we're not required to do those things, but if people need to be successful, they are gonna spend the money to be successful if they have it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Currently, I have spent $36,948.36, which includes my build every year because I am not going to pay for a removable like uh shipping container.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I'm having a contractor build and break down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: We tried to make as many pieces reusable as possible, but every year I will be throwing away lumber and paying for new because that was simply the cheapest option.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: But every year it'll cost me anywhere from eight to ten thousand dollars to rebuild plus another 500 to a thousand to pull away what I've built the previous year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So in the end, it's gonna cost me six six or sixty-eight thousand nine hundred and forty-eight dollars and twenty-six cents for four years, which is seven over like seventeen and a half thousand dollars a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I have to uh sell outside in those six months $1,149, $15 cocktails, $5,745 $3 hot dogs, uh, $907 burgers, or $2,872 $6 happy hour drinks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And that is between the hours of four that I open at midnight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And that is if it doesn't rain, or you know, some kind of weather incident because umbrellas simply just do not protect people, and that is within that very short warm period.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Um I just want to you know understand like we aren't rolling in money here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I'm not rolling in money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I had to forego paying myself a few times to make sure that I could afford outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And I am very entrenched in this community, and I'm very entrenched in advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And if it's hard for me, then I can tell you it is hard for so many people because I know the rules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I was the first bar that my community board saw.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And I can tell you that they weren't educated in a way that I had hoped that they had been.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: They were understanding, they were gracious, they listened, and in the end it worked out great, but I was expecting them to know more when I walked in that room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I shouldn't be the most educated person on the program in that room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: It's I should be coming in and expecting them to know what's going on, and then I can add to what I'm planning on providing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So, you know, we're not all millionaires, we're not all rolling in money, we're a neighborhood spot, and we want support, and we want support from you, and we want support from DOT.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Um, so I just hope that we can figure out a program that makes sense for people that are small and not just the people that have you know 40, 50 seats outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Again, I have 21.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So thank you so much for listening, and I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: My name's Robert Guarino.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: I'm a partner in New York City's chef-driven Group.

chapters.summary: Robert Marino, partner in the New York City Chef Group, testifies about the negative impact of changed sidewalk clearance rules on his Hell's Kitchen restaurant, Nizza, which reduced outdoor seating by 40%.
chapters.title: Robert Marino Testimony: New York City Chef Group

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh I'm here today to talk about one of our restaurants, a restaurant called Nietzsche in Hell's Kitchen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: As a group, we've operate we've operated, we do operate 16 restaurants all in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Many of those have been in business over 20 years, some as long as 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: And we uh believe strongly in um bringing bringing business out to the street and and the positive benefits that that has for the community and walkability and livability and safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Um so it's been a big part of what we do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh over the years, as we've analyzed commercial spaces before signing a lease, the first things we look at are is liquor license possible and is sidewalk cafe possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh and if I the answer to either one of those is no, we continue looking uh because it really is a vital part of our business model.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Um I think um with with the new program right now and the new regulations, I think a lot of what's been lost is some of the um very detrimental changes specifically to the sidewalk law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh so in Hell's Kitchen, we have three restaurants uh in the ground floor of the Film Center building between 44th Street and 45th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: That's that stretch of Ninth Avenue's only commercial building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: We have the corner of 44th, the corner of 45th, and then a little restaurant called Nietzsche in the middle of the block.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: That restaurant has has only 50 seats inside and always had 20 uh 24 seats outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh so by far our busiest months of the year were April, May, June, you know, July, and August, as the restaurant, you know, grew in size by 50%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Um, so we were very surprised when as we started applying, and we're not eligible for roadway in in any of these three restaurants because we're on the busy stretch of the avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Um, so we were you know, we assumed, okay, we'll go back to our existing, you know, permitted approved, you know, sidewalk setup that we've operated for 20, you know, for uh for 20 years and 15 years, and things will will be fine.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh we were shocked when we applied for NITSA to see that clearances had changed, and now we went from 24 seats to 14 seats.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: That might not sound like much, but you know, it's it's a 40 percent, you know, decrease in seats.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: That's the difference between having one server outside or two servers outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh it's you know, over the course of a busy day, lunch and dinner, that's you know, could be 50 people, you know, less people that we serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Um so we're really surprised that the sidewalk program specifically did not take into consideration the the original approved plans and basically changed clearances, and in many cases, especially in in uh in midtown, it it um has been very detrimental.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Uh and so we've you know, we we take a lot of pride in in having done business in Hell's Kitchen and Ninth Avenue for for 20 plus years, and we're happy that it's busy and thriving and people out there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: We think our are doing business outside does a lot for the safety of the neighborhood and does a lot to bring tourists that far west and to also serve the neighbors and people who live there and people who work in the theater community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: Um, and now making changes to the program I think is the exact opposite of of what we were uh hoping would happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Robert Guarino
utterances.text: And um, you know, I I encourage everybody to to spend the time thinking about roadway, which is a new program, but not to forget sidewalk, uh, which which has been operating for you know for 40 years across across the city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Great, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, I have a number of questions for this panel, and I know some of my colleagues do as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks the NYC Hospitality Alliance panel to quantify the economic and job losses experienced by restaurants due to the poor rollout of the permanent outdoor dining program.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions Hospitality Alliance on Economic and Job Losses

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to um acknowledge we've been joined by colleagues, Councilmember Ariola, Councilmember Narcis.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I want to, I guess a question first for the hospitality alliance.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Could you perhaps quantify what the economic losses?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know I heard your testimony, which was very compelling, but could you sort of writ large quantify what is the economic loss for restaurants due to the poor rollout of this program?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How many restaurants are gonna go out of business?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How many are gonna have to downsize, how many are gonna have to fire staff?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Could you try to give us a picture of that?

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So it's hard to give an overview just because the different sizes of the restaurants, but you can hear like from Megan, a small bar right there alone, she's spending 60 some odd thousand dollars over you know four years.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: In a case of Robert, where he's losing those seats, he's losing at least one server job there.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So there was some data I unfortunately don't have it in front of me now, but uh from the controller's office, about the millions of dollars in loss tax revenue uh to the city, and that was just actually based on I believe sales tax revenue.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: There's obviously the additional jobs, the payroll taxes that those jobs create, and the additional just general economic activity.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Um so the loss in revenue to the city, you know, is is millions.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Um the report I believe showed that the loss of jobs um is in the thousands, and for individual businesses, it's gonna depend on the size, but you could certainly hear just the cost alone uh many restaurateurs I know that have moved forward with the program are even it are not even sure if they're going to make money they'll be happy if they're able to break even at this point but could potentially lose thousands of dollars a year.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Let's not answer your question also let's not forget that during the emergency program it was well documented that 10,000 jobs were created or saved as a result of this vibrant outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: If that becomes 20% of what it was which is about where we're at now that's 80% you know you know of these additional jobs will be gone.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Tens of millions of dollars in tax revenues you know were secured you know for the city because of this vibrant outdoor dining as well as public survey after public survey especially in the boroughs said this is great.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We never had this before we love it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: It's added to our neighborhood um but the numbers we're seeing you know are are dramatic and and concerning so it's when you you in in Bronx for example as as he said there are 78 applications or or places approved 700 in the Bronx during during COVID.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Brooklyn there's 600 places now there was 3200 during COVID.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: In in Queens there was 2500 establishments during COVID only 250 now even Staten Island had 186 during COVID three now.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: During the w when we when you were a commissioner of consumer affairs and there were only 1200 or so sidewalk cafes, uh, an NYU study that came out during during COVID to show how wonderful the study you know, you know, this is added to to the city, both economically and socially, pointed out that there were 17 community boards that had no outdoor dining at all under the old law.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um, that neighborhoods of that low-income neighborhoods doubled the number of cafes that they had under the emergency program compared to the old law.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Neighborhoods where a majority uh or in minority restaurants also doubled the number of cafes that they had uh in the emergency compared to pre-COVID.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So we're losing if we lose those applications, this millions of dollars that the restaurants are using, losing, and the city is losing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, a couple follow-ups on that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, you're getting to the issue of equity.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin and the panel discuss the lack of language accessibility in the application process and the technical frustrations of the online-only system for immigrant business owners.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions Panel on Language Accessibility and Online Application Issues

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: One of the major policy issues that the original council bill was meant to address is equity, making sure that every single neighborhood had outdoor dining options.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Um the fact that so many neighborhoods now do not have them because of the rollout of and the all the different uh onerous rules and regulations that some of which you've identified.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: But I guess I I'm also very upset about the fact that DOT only has this application in English and in Spanish.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That's totally unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Are you hearing complaints from restaurant owners about that?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And can you talk a little bit about that?

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Yeah, we've we've heard about that.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We've heard about many problems with the application process, not only just language challenges and only having an online option, not having an actual window to go to and sitting down with a representative, um, but also another challenge about being able to save your application midway.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I'm not sure if DOT has changed the system yet, but a big problem was you couldn't save your application, you know.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: You know, so you basically go on, you get to one part.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Has this been Max?

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: You know if this has changed yet?

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Can you save?

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Yeah, so one of the challenges you go, you get, you know, a quarterway, a halfway through the process, and all of a sudden you're told you need some other document that you don't have.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So all of a sudden you stop the progress, you have to go find that document, and you come back and you have to start the process all over again.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So you can imagine those frustrations uh for anyone, but then if there's a language barrier, it just compounds those challenges.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: I can just add add to that, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: You know, a number, a number of the businesses that I interface with are first generation, zero generat generation immigrants, and I just can't underscore enough the expectation that they have that there is there ought to be a place, a physical place where they can go to get answers to questions, to hand in application materials.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: It's not, it's not passe or out of style to say that you know that there should be a place where people could actually bring paper and submit something, and that's been really been lost here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Absolutely, and that is why, and and I know you're aware of this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I objected to the original this original bill to this aspect of it that DOT should never have been given the jurisdiction over this program.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin and Robert Bookman discuss why the outdoor dining program should have remained under the jurisdiction of the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP) rather than the Department of Transportation.
chapters.title: Chair Menin and Robert Bookman Debate DOT vs. DCWP Program Management

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It should have always gone to DCWP, which has a licensing center right down the street that you can walk into that when people have issues, they can actually talk to someone to help them fill the paperwork out.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, why would we give this program to a transit agency?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why wouldn't we give the program to a licensing agency?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And we simply did not have those concerns, quite frankly.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And Rob, I know you can attest to this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You know, we worked together a long time on DCWP issues around the sidewalk cafe issues.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I'm very concerned about that aspect of this.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I I filed thousand applications over the years under the old I still call it consumer affairs, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's where I met my wife, and it was good.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We both worked at Consumer Affairs, so it's for me it's Department of Consumer Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I filed over a thousand applications.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You physically went there, it was it was the special application unit, not even a window, a special application unit because it was complicated.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There's plans, there's photographs, there's a lot of documents.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Somebody would sit down with you, you can make an appointment or wait, they would review it, and they would either uh say, No, this you need this, you need that, and then come on back and we'll accept the application, or you got everything here, stamped approved within five days.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They sent that on to the next step in you know uh in the process while they did their substantive review.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That was listen, that was a compromise.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The administration insisted on on it being uh DOT.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I think they you know they they've done the best job that they can be.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I just want to interrupt for a minute.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I just want to say it again.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The administration insisted that it be DOT.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We I I we I couldn't have been clearer personally with my objections.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know other colleagues had objections around DOT, and now I think we are all paying the price, and quite frankly, it needs to be remedied, and that's one of the goals of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Um, I do have one more question, and then I wanted to turn it over to the chair and the colleagues who I know have a number of questions.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks Robert Bookman to explain why only 67 restaurants have completed the full application process, pointing to DOT's misinterpretation of when the review clock starts.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions Robert Bookman on Low Approval Rates

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Can you specifically say why you believe that only 67 restaurants have completed the entire application process and revocable consent at this juncture?

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's because of the issue that I I raised, and uh let me try to be more clear about DOT's misinterpretation of what their how the clock starts.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The statute and in your packet that that your committee prepared, there's a there is a chart that DOT produced showing the various steps and how long they're supposed to take.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: First part of that chart is within five days of receiving a complete application, DOT sends it on to the next review agency's community board, and that starts the clock moving.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And if everybody lives with that clock, and the controller's office waives their review of the contract which they have, and the mayor's office waves their review of the contract, you know, which you know which they have.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's all new stuff in this in the in the statute that was not there before that you put in there.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The process could be quick, somewhat quick, three to four months.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: But DOT mistakenly, um and I call it a rookie mistake, didn't send these applications on within five days.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They did their entire substantive review first.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: When they were done with the back and forth with the applicant, uh, the objections, the corrections, revised plans, give me better pictures, all the normal things that an license agency has to do with the application like this.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Then they started the clock.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Well, that put the whole process behind the eight ball.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So there was no way when people were allowed to apply up to August 3rd, that by April they would be done with thousands of applications.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That the rest of the process would be done with thousands of applications because they didn't start the clock in August in July like they should have.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's the main thing.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: If they change that going forward, it shouldn't take as many months for new applications, but they haven't changed that yet, and they need to.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And in all fairness, I do want to say, because again, I don't want to point fingers here.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: On the other hand, the administration was the one who was pushing for all year-round outdoor dining, and DOT was as well.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And it was the council who said it should be you know, you know, it should be seasonal, and that's turned out to be a big mistake.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So there's mistakes to go around.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I I totally agree.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As someone who personally advocated for year-round outdoor dining, I totally agree.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I think it's completely unfair to restaurants that they have to basically bear the cost, have to put the equipment away.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It's absurd.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I was one who raised that issue.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know others did as well.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So let me turn it over to Chair Brooks Powers for her questioning.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, and thank you for your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Um I just had a couple of follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It was really helpful, particularly to hear from the businesses and what that direct impact was.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers asks Megan Rickerson to clarify the one-time versus recurring costs associated with hiring architects, lawyers, and contractors to build and store seasonal roadway setups.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions Megan Rickerson on Restaurant Setup and Storage Costs

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And Megan, hearing the dollar and cents was extremely helpful too.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I just wanted a little bit more clarity in terms of understanding that cost and how you perceive it going forward.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Um and so, for example, the architect fee, which while optional, I think is necessary based on some of the questions on the application.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Is that something that each time that you in the future go to reapply that you would then have to pay for, or is it just this one time?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Um this goes for four years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So it's it's one time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Okay, so thank you, council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Uh so one time, um, that fee was on its own, that was 1200.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I just put all of the lawyers' fees and architect fees all in one.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So that's one time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: But when I was talking about those numbers, that was what it would cost me to break even just on the build that doesn't like talk about my staffing, how much I have to pay people, it doesn't consider uh furniture, it doesn't consider any of those things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: It was strictly just in order to make it make sense for my business for those few months in those hours with perfect weather.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And in terms of and so I hear often like folks love the program to give the option to businesses, um, and you know, as it was said before, to expand even the the service you're able to give to your your customers.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How much just wanting to get an idea, dollar and sense again, and anyone can answer this.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers asks the panel about projected revenues under the new model and whether small businesses can realistically break even given the high costs and weather dependencies.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions Panel on Projected Revenues and Break-Even Points

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How much added revenue have you seen now or project with this new model?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Do you see yourself breaking even?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Is it still an added benefit?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: I think that's very much like remains to be seen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Like when I said I felt duped, it's because I don't know if the juice is worth the squeeze, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: Um, I don't know because I also didn't expect it to cost as much as it did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So it's really gonna be I have to get through this season and see where we're at.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: And you know, if next year I realize that I'm at a loss from operating, then I've just paid for four years up front, and I'm gonna use one of those because what's the point of operating at a loss and losing money on it?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Megan Rickerson
utterances.text: So I think this year is going to be very telling on how that goes.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And what are some of the recommendations from you know all anyone again can answer this for DOT to improve on the application process?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We've heard myself, um Chairman, and I know a number of my colleagues have heard that the application process has been very cumbersome and challenging.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
