utterances.text: And the fact that you're connected to the hospitality alliance makes you a more informed um business owner than some that may be out there trying to do it themselves.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so, me representing a community out in Southeast Queens, I'm not sure how many members are necessarily a part of the alliance themselves, but I'm I'm trying to understand what some of the recommendations could be that can ensure that this is an a truly accessible program for other business owners as well.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Yeah, I I want to say that I want to reiterate that, and I'm sure you'll hear it from DOT that to a large extent they were given a statute to deal with, and the statute required certain rules and regulations.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers and Robert Bookman discuss immediate recommendations to improve the program, including in-person support centers, quarterly fee payments, and simplified clearance rules.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers and Robert Bookman Discuss Recommendations for Program Accessibility

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We've we've got nothing but a positive working relationship with DOT from day one through yesterday.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um again, when it's it's not a finger pointing issue.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Part of the problem is, you know, we've we've we stated it needs to be an in-person, you know, that is something that could be corrected right away.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There needs to be an in-person place where people can have their papers reviewed and either accepted in in multiple languages with people there with multiple languages like they used to be a consumer affairs.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Uh that's something that could be done right away.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um they could start taking the annual fees quarterly that used to be done for 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's something they could start doing right away.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Those are policies.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They could start uh complying, you know, with the clear intent of the statute that five days they review the application.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: If all the papers are there, it's complete.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Send it on to the next part of the process, then do their review while the other while the community board is doing their review, you know, as well.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That always went on simultaneously.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Those are quick things that could be done.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Some things they have no control over.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The fact that there is a revocable consent, which makes the process complicated because it's not just a license going to a licensing agency and getting a license.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There's this consent.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: This consent has multiple steps, it has a contract, it has fees associated with it, you know, annual rent.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They're just administering that.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They that's something you've got to change.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And uh, and that would also make the process a lot faster if there was no consent.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So that's something that needs to be considered.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: I will say in the negotiation of all this, that was something that council staff was very much open to, getting rid of the consent for for outdoor dining, just like these other commercial uses of the public sidewalk.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: It didn't happen.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: But that's something that needs to be revisited.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So some things you can do, some things they can do, you know, some things they can't do.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: The enclosure is obviously very important as well because it gives them more time to monetize, you know, that space and pay for the fees.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: They're paying again a year-round uh uh license.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: They should be able to really use that space year-round, um, in addition to some of the other uh issues we mentioned as well.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's a rule, you know, that that could be changed by rule or a statute could over overrule that rule.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The other important thing is the clearances.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The people have touched on it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: For 40 years, the clearances on sidewalk cafes was very simple.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You didn't need to be a lawyer to understand it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: 50% of the sidewalk width or eight feet, whichever was greater.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Everybody could figure out right away how much space they can occupy for their cafe, and you know, they all know what the size tables and chairs are, they could pretty much figure it out before they even applied.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: DOT and came up with a very complicated map where every street in the city was color-coded.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And you now now it wasn't eight feet or 50% of the sidewalk, it was eight feet or ten feet or twelve feet, depending on which street you are on this color-coded map.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And then the way they measured the clearances changed dramatically.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Consumer affairs from the edge of the cafe to the curb.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You know, here and there, you know, some other, you know, if it was a fire hydrogen, he measures the fire hydrant.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They had all kinds of measurements to the complicated zone, anything in the in the zone you're measuring to that.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The bottom line is it not only made it more complicated, but that's what you're hearing from Mr.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Guarino and you know and and from uh some Sylvia's, is restaurants that had approved sidewalk cafes under those old rules for decades with no community problems, no passage problems on the sidewalk renewed every two, four years, all of a sudden had to shrink their sidewalk cafes and change the dot change the not only shrink the number of tables and chairs, but rather than having a nice clean line, they had to go in and out now, in and out, you know, to because of these crazy ways they decided to do clearances.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's something that's in their rules that has to change because that made it so confusing and it made it so unaffordable for some people to lose those tables and chairs that they just didn't apply.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So you're really seeing the decimation in the boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And roadway, so I would just add on the roadway, that sidewalk, but on the roadway cafes, obviously, you know, the seasonality is the problem.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: But there's also clearance requirements there as well that don't always make sense.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: In certain cases, based on where a restaurant is located on a corner, they sometimes have to provide, I believe, in certain cases, 50 feet, meaning that if you're located on one of these areas where you have to have 50 feet from a sign or a light before you can start your roadway cafe, you're basically cutting it into half or not having it at all.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I think daylighting, and I know there's some folks is more of a 10 or 15 foot.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: So I think if you're looking for safety measures, which we would support, uh, you could still give restaurants back more of the space and keep it safe with daylighting or other street safety features as well.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: But that's one of the things we're hearing about.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Also, certain no-standing zones or other types of signs and obstructions are also limiting restaurants' abilities.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Uh obviously, if you need to have space for a specific reason, you need to have it.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: But we found throughout the pandemic and now today, there's certain street signs or other obstructions that don't make much sense but are limiting the ability of a restaurant to have outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And again, not their fault, but the administration, and they work for the administration, so they had to listen, got other agencies involved that were not in the statute.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Everybody threw in their two cents when they were doing their rules, which created a hodgepodge of rules and regulations.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next, we have a couple of members that have questions for this panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We'll start with Councilmember Brewer, followed by Council Member Narcis.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you for such an informed panel.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You could run the city, all of you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks the panel about simplifying floor plan requirements and the challenges of technical measurement reviews conducted by volunteer community board members.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions Panel on Simplifying Floor Plans and Technical Reviews

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But in addition, what other language or other paperwork issues could be uh modified so that there are less problems for the applicant?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's question number one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The second is I'm a huge community board uh fan, um, and I do like uh items that go before the community board.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There is some wish that if every single requirement has been made for that applicant, that maybe they wouldn't have to.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I think I would just like to hear your opinion on that topic.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know that still the community board may want to have some input.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Uh and then also my question is, and this is something that I should know more about, but right now I believe it's a revocable consent, which is a very layered process.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so, would that be improved if it was a permit process, whatever that means, and I don't know exactly what that means.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So those are my three questions.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Sure, I can address some of those, uh, council members.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: So, in terms of the first question on paperwork requirements, one of the places where we're seeing a lot of applications get stuck is in a back and forth with the DOT license examiners over their floor plans.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: So the key thing that you've got to submit in one of these applications is a diagram showing not only what the tables and chairs layout is going to be, but you have to have a number of clearances which comply with their regulations.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: You heard about the clear path requirement already, which has been made complicated, but there's other clearance criteria as well.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: You have to be X number of feet from a fire hydrant, X number of feet from this, or from a poll, from a curb cut, et cetera, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: And a lot of applications are getting stuck there because it's just really complicated and confusing to get those clearance requirements correct.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: One of the things you did in the legislation was eliminate the requirement that there be an architect that submit the plans.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: And the purpose of that was because it was supposed to be that bar and restaurant owners shouldn't have to hire a professional if they could just even even hand-drawn in a clean, nice professional way, uh, or on a simple computer program, submit plans, it should have been easy.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: But what we're seeing is even though the requirement of hiring an architect has been eliminated, in reality, in the back and forth with the agency on the plans, it's almost like you got to be an architect in order to understand what they're saying and make the correction.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: So we're seeing over and over.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So, what would you suggest that it be instead?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How could we make it simpler?

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Clearance requirements need to be made simpler.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: I think some of the clearance requirements that you heard um heard about already are just too complicated.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what Andrew said, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Specifically the clear path.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: We need to go back, just to reiterate it because it's super important.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: We need to go back to the old DCA rule where your clear path, the amount of path on the sidewalk that's devoted to pedestrian flow is either eight feet or 50% of the sidewalk, whichever is greater.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: That's what you need to be at, that's what you need.

utterances.speaker: Max Bookman
utterances.text: Um on the community board process, um, we love community boards too, but one other aspect that's been really, really frustrating for applicants is they they think they're just applying to DOT, but then when the community board does get notified of the application, the community board hits the applicant with their own very lengthy, in some community boards, questionnaire series of questions, requirements, suggestions, um, and it confuses applicants.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And just and just on the on the community board point as well, I've just seen my experience.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: This is a member of a community board talking.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Yes, is doing it as we have it at CB7 on the upper west side, where people are going back and forth and trying to get into the technical aspects, and you hear the expertise, a lawyer that does this day in, day out, can still be confused by it.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, I hope we can hear from Mark Diller because he can simplify that for us.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Well, and then the revocable issue.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Get rid of it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: That's been uh an issue for me for decades, literally.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um I don't understand why a sidewalk newsstand that occupies 70 square feet as a permanent structure doesn't need a revocable consent, but uh movable tables and chairs do.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Uh and basically, when the sidewalk cafe law was passed in the 1970s, it did not.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You were a restaurant, you were able to apply, uh, there were there were basic clearances, and you got approved.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: 1980, two things happened.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The zoning overlay was added, and the revocable consent overlay was added.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We've now gotten rid of the zoning overlay, which is good for the boroughs, but we have not gotten rid of the rebel consent, it's a hard word for me, overlay.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And that overlay, there's no, you know, the you know, the council put it in the law.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: In my opinion, the council could take it out of the law.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Uh, you know, uh, you know, you you made it uh and you can take it away.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So uh again, there are multiple uses of the public sidewalk that don't have that rebel consent overlay.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So you go to an agency, it's generally consumer affairs, you file an application.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um the application ultimately goes through review process, if it gets approved, you get your license, you know, uh showing you're done.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Um, you know, you pay you do your renewals, they do the enforcement, whatever.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: The consent overlay makes it a multi-month, multi-layered, multiple expensive process.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: And there's no legal reason in my mind for it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There's certainly no ethical or moral reason, and there's no policy reason for it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: We whatever you need to do to get rid of it, you should get rid of it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: Whatever agency, whether it's DOT who's a licensing agency or consumer affairs, there's still going to be enforcement, there's still gonna be clearance.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Oh, well, the permit, there would be, yes.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: They'll have a permit, they'll have a license, which could still be taken away if you know three strikes and you're out.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: All that stays the same.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You just don't have this extra stuff.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: So there's no controller's office reviewing the same 20-page contract, literally 3,000 times.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You don't have the mayor's office reviewing the same boil plate 20-page contract 30 times.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: You know, 3,000 times.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: There's no reason for it.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: It's a whole bureaucracy that I don't know why it was created in 1980.

utterances.speaker: Rob Bookman
utterances.text: It's even before my time, but it's time is passed.

utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Council Member Robert Holden introduces Intro 857-A, explaining that the bill requires the Department of Sanitation to tow abandoned and inoperable vehicles within 72 hours of notification to reclaim public space.
chapters.title: Council Member Holden Introduces Intro 857-A on Towing Abandoned Vehicles

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Thank you, uh Madam Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Uh thank you, Chair Menem and Chair Um Bro Powers for the opportunity to speak on my bill.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Intro 8578.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: The intent of H uh Intro 857A is simple.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: It requires the Department of Sanitation to tow vehicles that are incumbents on our city streets within 72 hours of notification by NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: This legislation is not about tidying up our curbs, it's about reclaiming public space, ensuring safety, and restoring fairness to how our streets are used.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Every day, abandoned inoperable and improperly parked vehicles clog our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: They block bike lanes, bus stops, fire hydrants, and curb space desperately needed for everything from commercial deliveries to parking for residents.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: These abandoned vehicles turn our streets into graveyards for zombie cars and are a source of constant frustration for all of our constituents.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: This legislation sets a clear, reasonable time frame for action.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: It empowers the Department of Sanitation to act swiftly but fairly.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Clean, safe, and accessible streets are not a luxury.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: They're a big basic necessity in a city of over eight million people.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Let's not allow the bad actors who abandon vehicles to hold entire blocks hostages.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: I urge my colleagues to support this common sense legislation and put our public space back to public use.

utterances.speaker: Rafael Salamanca, Jr.
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
