chapters.summary: First Deputy Commissioner Margaret Forgione provides an overview of the permanent Dining Out NYC program, highlighting that over 2,500 restaurants are authorized to operate for the first permanent season.
chapters.title: Agency Testimony: DOT's Overview of the Permanent Dining Out NYC Program

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: I am Margaret Forgioni, First Deputy Commissioner of the New York City Department of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: With me today are Michelle Craven, Associate Deputy Commissioner for Cityscape and Franchises, and Rick Rodriguez, Assistant Commissioner for Intergovernmental and Community Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify on behalf of Mayor Adams and Commissioner Rodriguez on the city's outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: During the pandemic, New Yorkers came to enjoy and appreciate outdoor dining in new ways.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: It was not only a critical lifeline for many restaurants and small businesses when indoor dining was prohibited, but it contributed to the vibrancy of our neighborhoods and improved the dining experience for so many people, especially on warm sunny days.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: As the pandemic ended, New York City DOT worked closely with the City Council to make outdoor dining a permanent part of our city's streetscape, and we are pleased that the council did so when it passed Local Law 121 of 2023.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The law created seasonal roadway dining, preserved year-round sidewalk dining, and provided the framework for the future of outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Just as we did during the emergency program, DOT has worked hard to help as many restaurants participate in the program as possible.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Three weeks ago, DOT launched the first season of Dining Out NYC.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: There were over 2,500 restaurants authorized to operate, including every restaurant that applied by the August 3rd, 2024 deadline, and completed any required steps.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: While a new program and rules brings new realities for restaurants, we are encouraged that the program is already well over double the size of the city's pre pandemic program, is the largest outdoor dining program in the country, and has a similar participation rate to Paris, which also recently made elements of its outdoor dining program seasonal.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Our team has been working tirelessly to get us to this point, and we are proud of all that we have achieved so far.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Now to give some background on how we got here.

chapters.summary: First Deputy Commissioner Margaret Forgione outlines the history of outdoor dining in NYC, from the pre-pandemic DCWP program to the pandemic-era emergency program, and explains the regulatory balance of Local Law 121.
chapters.title: Agency Testimony: Historical Context and Regulatory Balance of Outdoor Dining

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: First, the pre pandemic sidewalk cafe program managed by DCWP, then the pandemic era era temporary outdoor dining program authorized by mayoral emergency executive orders, and now the permanent dining out NYC program created in response to Local Law 121 of 2023.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: With each iteration, we have built on lessons learned and took bold steps to reimagine the use of public space.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: They also had to comply with a building code, obtain a revocable consent through DCWP, obtain approval from city planning, and approval from the landmarks preservation commission, depending on the location of the restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: These regulations were confusing and restrictive, creating barriers to small businesses trying to participate in the program, especially in the outer boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Then in 2020, the COVID-19 pandemic brought us challenges unlike any we had seen before.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The necessity of closing indoor dining for most of 2020 meant that restaurants were particularly impacted by the pandemic, losing thousands of staff and in many cases closing permanently.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Balancing public health guidelines and the need to save the city's beloved restaurant industry, the city rapidly created a temporary open restaurant program so New Yorkers could safely dine outside.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The program reimagined the use of public space to bring restaurant dining to streets and sidewalks across the city.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: During the peak of the pandemic, the program had between 6,000 and 8,000 restaurants participating and saved 100,000 jobs.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The temporary program was free for restaurants, suspended most pre-existing sidewalk cafe requirements, and allowed restaurants to self-certify that they met the temporary program's requirements, all with the goal of encouraging as many restaurants to participate as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: While the program was overwhelmingly popular, it also brought about numerous quality of life concerns around noise and sanitation, among other issues.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Thus, when planning the permanent outdoor dining program, DOT worked to implement a program built around lessons learned from both the DCWP Sidewalk Cafe program as well as the temporary open restaurants program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The goal that we shared with the council was to strike a balance between the programs of the past, loosening requirements from the pre-pandemic program as much as possible to encourage wider participation while updating regulations to address quality of life concerns.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The program has significantly lower fees than the pre-pandemic sidewalk cafe program, with rates varying by location and setup size.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Simultaneously, a zoning text amendment was also removed, also removed the pre-pandemic program's numerous geographic restrictions, allowing the restaurants to participate in areas of the city where outdoor dining was not allowed previously.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: With the transition to the first year of dining out NYC, we are proud of the work the agency has done so far.

chapters.summary: First Deputy Commissioner Margaret Forgione details DOT's extensive outreach efforts, application statistics, and the implementation of conditional approvals to allow roadway cafes to operate by the April 1 deadline.
chapters.title: Agency Testimony: Outreach, Application Statistics, and Conditional Approvals

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: DOT conducted extensive outreach, including 44 webinar presentations with question and answer sessions for restaurants, repeated email communications to every business with a food service establishment permit, and over 15,000 POM cards distributed in person.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We also did extensive engagement with the news media and via social media to raise awareness about the program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: DOT has received nearly 3,900 applications from over 3,200 restaurants, and our goal is to help all of these restaurants participate in the program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: When an application is incomplete or includes errors, our staff doesn't just reject the application, we work with applicants to help them achieve approval.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Our application is also available in 12 languages, a concern that we heard raised today.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: To expedite the lengthy application process required by Local Law 121 and other requirements of the city charter, and to allow as many restaurants to be up and running by April 1st as possible, DOT issued conditional approvals to restaurants applying to operate a roadway cafe that had already gone through the DOT review, community board review, and had a public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Thanks to this effort, the vast majority of restaurants that applied for a roadway cafe by August 3rd deadline were approved to operate.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: In addition, all restaurants that applied for a sidewalk cafe prior to the August 3rd deadline have been allowed to operate.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We are thrilled that outdoor dining is now a permanent part of the city's streetscape, and we want to work with the council to make this program as successful as possible.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: This includes listening to the concerns of restaurants about how to expand participation and to make the application process faster and easier.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We are just over three weeks into the first dining out NYC season, so I'm sure there will be more lessons learned and additional feedback to consider.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We look forward to discussing lessons from this first season with the council and other stakeholders so that we can work together to make outdoor dining a success for many years to come.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify before you today.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We now welcome any questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Great, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So a number of questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, first of all, I I really want to drill down on this number of the 67 businesses with full approval.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin questions DOT on why only 67 businesses have received full approval, challenging the agency's claim that the program is going well.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Low Approval Rates and Application Backlogs

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Because the goal of the hearing is obviously to try to fix this backlog and figure out what we can do to improve this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why is it that only 67 businesses have received this full approval?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, I'll start out by going through some of the numbers and then Michelle will add if I've I've missed anything.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: First and foremost, we want to emphasize that 80% of the program's applicants are now able to be up and running as of April 1st.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So the bottom line is that 80% of restaurants can operate as we speak.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So that for us is the most key factor here.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We want to get every restaurant operational as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So does that give restaurants a certainty that they need?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: They're outlaying a lot of expenses on this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So let me explain that a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And maybe I'll run through some of the numbers, Chair, if that's good, and then I'll get your question.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we've had 3,922 total applications, and that is from about 3,200 different food service establishments.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Of those applications, 774 are roadway, 1754 are sidewalk, and 697 are both.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Of that group of applications, we have 511 that were either withdrawn by the applicant or were denied because they were in a bus stop or they were at a hydrant or something of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So that leaves us with about 3,411.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Of that 3,411, 2,603 are able to operate either because they have gone through the process, or if their sidewalk they applied between before August 3rd, or if their roadway, they've gone through so much of the process that we know their application is correct and they've already had a public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So to answer your question, the people that have been able to set up now, roadway in particular, we have completely reviewed every aspect of their application and they're all correct.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So they won't be in the situation where they've set something up, and then upon the final license, we're gonna say, Oh, what you put out there is incorrect.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: That that will not happen.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: They know exactly what they need to put out.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: In the meantime, we have the 1800 sidewalk setups also operating, and we've been very clear with all of them that they should do so, and that we'll keep working through the process in the meantime.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What happens to the 20%?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So the 20%, let me tell you about that exactly.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks DOT about the status of the remaining 20% of applications and suggests establishing walk-in licensing centers in every borough to assist struggling business owners.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Pending Applications and Walk-In Licensing Centers

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we have a number that we're in active discussions with or are non-responsive.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And so what we found, and Michelle can explain this a little bit further, is that as we as we talked about earlier, some of the restaurants and the hospitality lines mentioned, a lot of the restaurants, because we did not require an architect or an engineer to prepare their drawings, we got some quite substandard drawings.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So what we've had to do in those cases is go back and forth with the restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And what we quickly learned is that the restaurants, they're very busy, they're running a business, or they don't have the expertise.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: What we have done rather than tell them your measurements are wrong, your sidewalk distances are totally incorrect.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: What we have done is we have corrected those errors for them, sent it back to them, asked them to review, and then we're keeping their application moving.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Of the ones that we're waiting to approve now, what we're seeing is some of them are not responding to us, or some of them may choose to withdraw, but they're not telling us they don't want to participate in the program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we are and we're doing everything we can to get to them.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've offered to come out to their restaurant, we've offered a Zoom call to go through the plans.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um they can come to us.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We're we're open to any of those things that the restaurants mentioned earlier, but we can't necessarily force every restaurant to engage with us if they're not using.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why wouldn't you set up a walk-in licensing center in every single borough to make it easy for restaurants to walk in, get their paperwork processed, and get their questions answered?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Chair, I'd rather even make it easier.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We'll go to the restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have gone to restaurants and we've offered restaurants to come to them.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: What we've heard from most of them is they're super busy, so they actually prefer the Zoom option, the remote option, where we call up the plan on the screen, they're in the restaurant, we're in the office, and we do that together.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We change the plan, fix it, and move along.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That it that sounds great.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It's just not what we're hearing in the field.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It totally contradicts uh complaints that I'm getting to my office that I know that my colleagues are getting that the hospitality alliance is getting where people feel that it's stuck in bureaucratic red tape and that they're not hearing back from DOT.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So there seems to be a total disconnect between what you're saying and what we're hearing from restaurants and the hospitality alliance.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, well, actually, the folks who were here before us, a lot of them indicated an excellent response out of DOT, and we are returning all the calls within a day or so.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks DOT why only 3,200 restaurants applied for the permanent program compared to the 13,000 that participated during the pandemic peak.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Low Restaurant Application Rates

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've responded to every email, we don't have a backlog, so we're actually being very responsive.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And if you know of any businesses or your colleagues that are having trouble, let us know, and we will have someone hand hold their hand immediately and go to them if they want us to go to the we will definitely do that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Now, why do you think so few restaurants are applying?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You mentioned the little over 3,000 statistic, but there were 13,144 businesses participating in outdoor dining at the end of the city's temporary restaurants program.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So that we sort of have an abysmal application rate.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why is that?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we actually think we've had a good application rate.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: At the height of the program during COVID, we had about six to eight thousand restaurants operating.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The 13,000 number is people that may have signed up, they may or may not have ever even used the option.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: They may have stayed in business, they may have gone out of business.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We didn't require a restaurant to tell us if they were setting up or not.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we think the number we're talking about is about six to eight thousand, and we have at this point about four thousand applications.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And just as a um, just to mention that a comparable program to New York is probably Paris, and they have not seen that percentage of people signing up between the temporary and the permanent program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we actually think we're doing pretty well.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: However, we're not resting on our laurels.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We'd love the dining out program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We want more people to participate as we get through this first year, and we're gonna have time to really focus on even more outreach.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We will go out to communities, we'll work with any of the council members, boards, whoever, and we will try to solicit more people and get them in the program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And Michelle, do you want to add anything?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I was just gonna add that we are seeing new applications every day, so the numbers keep going up regularly.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why is the application only in English and in Spanish?

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin questions DOT on the language accessibility of the application, and DOT clarifies that the application is now available in 12 languages.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Language Accessibility of the Application

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That's not correct, it's in 12 languages.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, because we're hearing from restaurants, we heard that directly.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So it's on the you're saying it's it's absolutely in 12 languages, because we heard complaints about language access, so that those complaints are incorrect.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Yes, it launched in English and Spanish.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: That's what our IT department was able to get ready at the time, but we have increased it to 12 languages since long.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: When did you increase it?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I do not know specifically, but we can find out for you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We literally were hearing as of like the last couple weeks that it wasn't.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So if this is something new that was done, that's great, but that is not what we've been hearing.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I'm gonna say that it was before the last few weeks, but we'll find out and get more clarity.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What can be done in terms of the complaints that you heard from the previous panel?

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks DOT how they plan to address the myriad of complaints raised by restaurant owners regarding the confusing and expensive application process.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Addressing Restaurant Owner Complaints

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: There are a myriad complaints.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How do you recommend that the agency fix those complaints?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Everything from the revocable consent portion to the excessive fees to the backlog to the delay to the issue around architectural drawings.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, we heard how are you proposing that you can fix all of these different issues?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, so some of that comes back to a complicated process that comes back to both the local law and the city charter.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So I think there are discussions for us to have with the council on some of this.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um but I think you know what we want to do is get through this first year or so and see how restaurants are doing once the program settles in a little bit and then take stock of where we are and um keep making improvements.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But but maybe Michelle will talk to you a little bit about some of the measures we have taken so far to make sure that the process is as smooth as possible.

chapters.summary: Associate Deputy Commissioner Michelle Craven explains DOT's dedicated plan review process, outreach efforts, and how reviewers work directly with restaurants to correct substandard drawings.
chapters.title: Michelle Craven Explains DOT's Plan Review Process and Outreach Efforts

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So we're gonna 213.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So one thing I guess from the very beginning, we assign every application to a dedicated plan reviewer.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: They work with the restaurants directly to um make sure the applications are complete to make sure that the site plans include what they need to include.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I will point out, just because there was a lot of discussion about um site plans and the kind of review that needed to happen of the site plans up front, some of the site plans we receive are like they don't have any dimensions.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It may not have a street name on it, like a very, very, very basic.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And I think we found that if we sent those to the community boards, the community boards would be very unhappy with us for sending that little detail about a particular site plan to them.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And so that is why we work with the restaurants very closely up front to try to get those site plans into complaint into compliance and to make sure that the restaurant itself, the the outdoor dining setup would be in compliance.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um we try to we work to make every application viable to make sure it gets approved.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Anything that we think we can get it approved, we're going to try to get it approved.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We offer, as the first deputy commissioner said, we have a lot of Zoom and team sessions with people.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We take remote calls so that we can be online updating the site plans for the restaurants instead of asking them to do it themselves.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We can do it in one fell swoop.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: They can take any measurements if they need to, they'll be at the restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We take care of that right away.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We can make site visits if we need to, we have done that.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: If there are minor issues with an application that maybe don't affect the site plan or really affect the application that severely, we'll send it to the community board right away rather than hold it up.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: If for roadway applications, I'll say because the community boards get 30 days to look at it, and the lead time for a public hearing notice is approximately a month.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We will basically start the advertising process for a public hearing as soon as we send it to the community board so we can hold the public hearing right away after the community board's time has run out.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what type of specific outreach are you doing to restaurants about the program?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And if you can be specific by borough, because we're seeing a real equity issue.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We're seeing a lot in Manhattan, but not in some of the other boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I want to understand what's happening.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have done over the last year, year and a half, um, a lot of outreach.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've worked with the Department of Small Business Services in particular to help us reach a lot of the restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have contacted every single restaurant that has a food permit what is the food service establishment permit.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've done 15 webinars with community boards, 29 webinars with for QA sessions with restaurants, the bids, the hospitality groups, other city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've done a tremendous amount of email blasts with dining out program information with links to the website.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've done a number of in-person presentations, tabling a business events.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We've presented at New York City Interagency Task Force meetings, 13 press releases since 2024, and distributed over 15,000 POM cards with information.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And our inspectors have gone to every restaurant that participated in the temporary program twice in order to give them information on the permanent program to make sure they understood it.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: That's just a sampling of it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I don't have it broken down by borough.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin questions DOT on the flexibility of sidewalk clearance requirements and how scaffolding rules impact outdoor dining setups.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Sidewalk Clearances and Scaffolding Rules

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We've heard from a lot of restaurants that they're recommending that the city allow for greater flexibility for safe sidewalk cafe clearances, and we heard that issue brought up before, as well as DOT scaffolding requirements and getting clarification on that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Have you taken any of those two issues and would you take them moving forward and clarify them so that we're not putting this onerous burden on the applicant?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So, Chair, are you speaking about our clearance requirements and are we willing to revisit those?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So what we have done, and you know, things have changed a lot.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um, you know, I heard the the folks testifying before us talking about how they've had certain clearance requirements dating back to DCWB's program for decades.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The needs of the city are constantly changing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And as we develop a permanent outdoor dining program, we felt it was very critical to look at what we need now in the city.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Everyone here is aware we have a ton of competing demands for both sidewalk and roadway in the city.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But focusing on sidewalk in particular, sidewalks first and foremost, are to be able to get people safely to move from one place to the other.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we've developed, and Michelle can talk a little bit more about it, but we have developed some clear path requirements very analytically, looking at land use such as density of the area, height of the buildings, land, you know, land use with hospitals, subway stations, etc., to gauge how much sidewalk space is necessary to safely and comfortably move people.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So that is the basis for our sidewalk clearances, and we've even accommodated those a little bit further and given a little a few more feet in for each of those categories to dining out.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But we feel it's very important to be very measured as we approach the permanent program and give a fair amount of space to pedestrians moving on the sidewalks.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And that's not to say that's not something we won't want to revisit in the future.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And and any other details you want to add?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I'll just add for aside from the pedestrian clear path, which is obviously very important, there are clearance requirements for other elements in the sidewalk, whether they're fire hydrants, subway stairs, bus shelters, whatever else.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: A lot of those objects already have certain clearances that have to be maintained for them so that they can be used appropriately.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: But we had a series of interagency meetings, I think starting in 2021, to talk through everybody's infrastructure, the needs that are out there, sister agencies, MTA, utility companies to figure out what's sort of the right size for the different clearances.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And we tried to be as flexible as possible for the restaurants to get as many restaurants operating as we could.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And the fiscal year 2025 November plan added 176,000 for an additional eight headcount positions to process outdoor dining applications.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks DOT if the agency has filled the additional staff positions funded in the FY 2025 budget to process outdoor dining applications.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Staffing Levels and Budget Allocations

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Have all of those positions been filled?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Do you think that you need more staff to address the backlog?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: I don't think we need more staff, so maybe I'll just talk about that for a moment.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have obviously this is our biggest year in order to address the applications that are coming in this year.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have our 24 staff that are funded as part of the program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We also trained some other staff within the department and put them on loan into this program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And we also hired some consultants for a number for several months in order to help review applications.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we think that the staff in the program is probably the right number going forward, but we're also prepared to take measures to bring in more staff if we ever need to.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I just think when you have the number of complaints you have, and we know that there have been tremendous number of complaints, despite what your testimony is saying, there are enormous amounts of complaints.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: There is a huge backlog.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It also, I do want to point out that records show that from 2017 to 2019, it took DCWP an average of 177 days from the filing of an application to the approval of a sidewalk cafe license.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And the one of the whole intents of this law was to try to speed up this process, but now we're seeing that many applications are still in this backlog.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So why do you think that eight staff members is enough to address this program?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, the reason the program is taking so long is really because of the steps in the process coming back to some things required in the city charter, other things required in the local law.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin questions DOT on the average time it takes to process an application and requests examples of applications that were approved within a few weeks.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Questions DOT on Application Processing Times

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: You know, we're giving counsel, we're giving community board review time.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have a public hearing, we have to notice the public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So they're each of those things that I just rattled off, and Michelle can get into them in detail if we want to talk about them.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Those things are just months, those add together and form months.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So it is less, I would say, if we have an active restaurant that's engaging with us in the back and forth, we can get the application final very quickly in a matter of a few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But it's really all these other steps in the process that add on so much time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Can you give us examples if you don't have them now?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Could you send to both of the chairs examples of restaurants where it's taken a couple weeks from start to finish?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Because I would like to talk to those restaurants because that is absolutely not what we're hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So if you could get that to us by the end of the week, I'd like to have a list of all restaurants where it took a couple weeks start to finish for the examples of that, sure.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yes, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Um, the seasonal nature of the program, which we know the council had that requirement.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks DOT if the agency is open to legislative amendments that would allow for a year-round roadway dining program to reduce storage burdens on restaurants.
chapters.title: Chair Menin Asks DOT About Year-Round Roadway Dining Flexibility

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I personally objected to that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I think this should be a year-round program.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Is DOT open to uh an amendment where it would be a year-round program so that we're not putting again the burden on restaurants to buy all this equipment, store the equipment, then reassemble the equipment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, we'd be happy to engage in discussions about that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, I have a number of more questions, but I want to be respectful of my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So let me turn it over to Chair Brooks Powers.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers asks DOT to walk through the step-by-step review process for sidewalk cafe licenses and revocable consents, including community board timelines.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions DOT on Sidewalk Cafe Review Process

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Hi, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Great to see you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Um, I'm gonna start with the application process issues, um, just picking up on some of what Chair Menon had mentioned and what we heard from some of the businesses earlier.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you walk through DOT's approval process for applicants that submitted a license and revocable consent application?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Specifically, what does the preliminary review from DOT include and how long does it take?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So I'll walk you through for both the sidewalks and the roadways because there are separate processes as set up in the legislation.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, the preliminary review process, we receive the application, including the site plan photographs, supporting documents.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We review to make sure we have everything that we need.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We review the site plan to make sure that it is complete and accurate and includes the information that we need to submit it to the community board for proper review.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um so we look at the site plan itself, we look at the photographs that are submitted, we go onto cyclomedia or street view to get a sense of the lay of the land.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, and if there are issues with the site plan, then we work with the applicant to um improve the site plan and get it into good shape.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think one thing we found early on is that we would send comments back and ask the restaurants to sort of update their site plan themselves and come back to us, and we realized that that was not efficient at all, and so that's when we started up these Zoom meetings, so we could just do everything online to the extent we could and basically redraw it ourselves.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, but so that's the the preliminary review process.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um then we submit the applications to the community board.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: The uh this is for sidewalk to the community board, the borough president, and the affected city council member.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: The community boards have 40 days to decide whether or not they're gonna hold a public hearing or send comments back to us.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, depending on how things go with the community board, we may be required to hold a public hearing at ZOT.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um so then we either approve the application, approve it with a modification, or deny.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, if we approve or approve with modification, then we send it here to the city council.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: The council has 45 days to decide whether or not to call the application to a vote.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: If it makes it through the city council successfully, then we notify the applicant that it's been approved and we reach out to them to execute the revocable consent agreement, um, send us insurance and then the annual fee, then it comes back to us, we execute, it goes to Mox, and then it goes, it's entered into FMS and goes to the comptroller.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: For the roadway cafes, um it starts off the same way with the application submission, the preliminary review.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers asks DOT to detail the review process for roadway cafes, including public hearing requirements and charter-mandated advertising timelines.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions DOT on Roadway Cafe Review Process

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We forward thing we forward the application to the affected community board and city council member who have 30 days to comment and submit comments to us.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We then hold the public hearing, which, as I mentioned before, um this public hearing is a charter requirement.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It requires three weeks of advertising notices in the city record as well as two newspaper ads that have to be paid for by the applicant according to the city charter.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, but we try to overlap the public hearing notice advertising period with the community board review time to try to make it as efficient as possible.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We hold our public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: If everything goes well, then we approve the application, send it out for execution, comes back, goes to Mox, the compstroller.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then we issue the license.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And how are you prioritizing the pending applications?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So what we did first was we looked at the roadway applications in order to get them moving because we knew that any sidewalk applicant that had applied by the August 3rd deadline was going to be able to operate.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we took care of the roadway ones first and started them on the process, and then we had moved on to the sidewalk applications next.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Aside from inadequate site plans, what are some common issues applicants face in the preliminary review stage?

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers asks DOT about the most common issues applicants face during the preliminary review stage, such as payment delays and missing paperwork.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions DOT on Common Preliminary Review Issues

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, so several things.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Again, as you mentioned, the response times from restaurants at the beginning, then we we often wait for payment for the public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We're having a hard time getting that payment secured in some cases from restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And how much is it?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It about $800 or so.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And then the last thing is like the remaining paperwork at the end to finalize the process.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Did DCWP provide any advice or guidance to DOT on management of the sidewalk cafe program before Local Law 121 transferred it to DLT?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: I know we had um several meetings with them to talk over their program, their experiences, and the process.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We've also the legislation required us to hold a quarterly task force meeting with DCWP and the Department of City Planning, and we've been doing that since the legislation passed.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Because I believe I've I've been told that I guess the rules in the current program are not, you know, a mere image of what it was prior to local 121.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so having the um learn lessons from the previous program, I'm wondering how DLT use that information to determine what the rules would be for this current program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So I think again, the sticking point are some of the requirements that are just in the charter and the local law.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So even understanding some of those issues didn't necessarily mean we could avoid them.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So in terms of the pedestrian clear path, I think the first deputy commissioner alluded to this a little bit, but we did um this the agency conducted or released the pedestrian mobility plan that did a modeling analysis throughout the city to identify streets that have higher pedestrian traffic versus streets with lower pedestrian traffic and recommended pedestrian clear paths for different types of corridors based on those usage uses.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers questions DOT on how sidewalk clearance requirements were determined and how the agency coordinates with utilities and sister agencies.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions DOT on Sidewalk Clearances and Utility Coordination

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We used the guidance in the pedestrian mobility plan to set the clear path in this program.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We did make some adjustment because we were trying to balance getting more restaurants to operate, as many restaurants to operate as we can versus providing adequate clearances for pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And so for the larger corridors, we reduced the recommended requirements by several feet.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: But that's where we got these numbers for the um for the pedestrian clear path.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How did DOT determine the proper distance between sidewalk cafes and other street elements such as subway infrastructure, tree beds, mailboxes?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So we held a series of interagency meetings for a long period of time to um and we reviewed the existing clearances that were out there, both that were listed in the old DCWP program as well as clearance requirements that other programs have for their infrastructure to try to come up with the most appropriate uh clearances here.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And that was sister agencies, the MTA, ConEd, National Grid, anybody who might be touching the street.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay, and my last set of questions is gonna focus on the resources for restaurants to be compliant.

chapters.summary: Chair Selvena Brooks-Powers asks DOT for an update on federal funding secured for outdoor setups in Queens and the usage of the online Marketplace Directory.
chapters.title: Chair Brooks-Powers Questions DOT on Federal Funding and the Marketplace Directory

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you provide a status update on the federal funding that was secured by Congresswoman Grace Meng for setups in New York 6?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um, yes, one moment, please.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, so SBS is managing this project, but we're working very closely with them and also the Queen's Chamber of Commerce.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And her funding will secure about 60 setups that will be distributed to interested establishments within her district.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So to date, three of those setups have been installed.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: 14 are going to be installed in the next two weeks.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And then there's engagement happening with 32 other restaurants who are interested in participating.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you please provide the committees with an update on DOT's marketplace directory?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Yes, so we're very proud of the marketplace directory.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We have lots of different businesses who are sharing their ability to provide the items that are compliant items in our program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The companies can construct, they can assemble or disassemble, they can design the setups, they can move them, they can store them, all of that.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we have heard back from businesses that they have found this to be a very useful tool to do some one stop shopping for their dining out setting.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How many licenses have taken licensees have taken advantage of the services provided through the marketplace?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Yeah, we haven't required them to report back to us on that, so I don't have a number for you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, we've got a number of colleagues uh with questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Um council member Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: She's not a good thing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I, for one, always glad to see Margaret For Gion.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's me.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks DOT if revocable consents can be replaced with a simpler permit process and how the agency coordinates with other city departments.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions DOT on Simplifying Floor Plans and Technical Reviews

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I like the commissioner's okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I can deal with him.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I'll take Margaret any day of the week.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um, first of all, I think we all want to know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know this is the general question, but what the process is long.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are you do you have some suggestions that could make it shorter?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know that in some cases when it's a perfect storm, it sounds like you can do it in weeks.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But are there some suggestions you would have to make it shorter?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I guess picking up on the uh chairman is does revocable consent have to exist, or could we do a permit?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So Michelle will comment a little bit more about revocable consent.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: It's part of the charter, and there's a question on whether.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So when I t testify tonight at the charter, the mayor's charter, I will say get rid of the goddamn thing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, and that might entail a referendum, but I'm gonna have Michelle explain that because she understands it best.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But in terms of all of the other requirements, we would want to talk that over with the council and decide do we want to have some of the review periods be less, for example?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Those are things that we would want to talk about further.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And we're open.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We want to make it um as smooth as possible.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But Michelle, do you want to explain about it?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I said that thing.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I would very much like to explain.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think a lot of this was um comes out of discussions from the city law department, so I don't think we'd get into too much detail right here.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um but my understanding, because we were trying to streamline the process as much as possible, sort of going into the legislation.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: My understanding is that the consent was the only option at the time.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think we would be happy if there is another way to do this to talk about a different option.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think if you're gonna go to the CRC, feel free to feel free.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Added it to my list already.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um, but that's where we I think we'd be happy to talk more, but probably offline.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um, what other agencies do you work with?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Fire, sanitation, et cetera, and is there coordination?

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks DOT about the level of coordination with the Fire Department, Sanitation, and City Planning through the quarterly task force.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions DOT on Interagency Coordination

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know you mentioned some of the other agencies too, SBS.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Uh how does that coordination work?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it through that task force that you mentioned, or are there other ways that they coordinate?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So we coordinate as needed, I think, um, throughout the process.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We work with SBS quite frequently actually for outreach to the restaurants, um, and in terms of the MANG funding and other um issues like that the first deputy commissioner mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Um we work with the health department, obviously, in terms of making sure we have the appropriate um eligible restaurants, um, to make sure everything is clear there.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We work with the fire department is needed, sanitation is needed.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Obviously, we've talked to DCWP and city planning.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's you know, as things come up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it's it's an ad hoc as opposed to something that everybody has to participate in.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Aside from the quarterly task force meeting, I would say it's more ad hoc.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um the other question I have is the website.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So the website now I look at it and it has correctly who applied, etc.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks DOT if the legal or illegal status of outdoor dining setups can be clearly indicated on the agency's public website.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions DOT on Adding Setup Status to the Website

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But it doesn't, we get complaints of we don't know if they're legal or not.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, I'm like the you know, the uh person who spent a great deal of time on the illegal smoke shops, as you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we don't know if they're legal or illegal.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It doesn't say that on the website.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is there some way that that could be added?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because people are bringing us, we I don't know if it's legal or not.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We we can call, but that'll be something that I think people would appreciate.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: If you give them a summons, can that be listed, etc.?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, so the website will be continually updated, but in the meantime, if there is a question about any setup, if you call us, we are I know I can call you, but I'm just saying, but anybody can call 311 or nyc.gov and we will tell them what the dimensions are supposed to be and whatnot.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But as we continue along with the program and the final um approvals, the website will be more and more updated and more useful to your constituents.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: About those that are not legal also?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: If they're not in the website, they're probably illegal.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But then it needs to say that because people don't know that it needs it needs that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what happened during the smoke shops.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: People didn't know if they were legal or illegal.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks DOT how the agency coordinates street cleaning and road paving around active roadway dining setups.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions DOT on Street Cleaning and Paving Coordination

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They had no idea.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, you and I might know because of the insignia in the window, but believe me, they didn't know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can we be a second round?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have more questions.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't want to take other time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Go ahead, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um the other question I have is just in terms of the you heard earlier about the street cleaning.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um obviously that was a concern before.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So what how would you know road paving or other issues?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are we gonna make sure that there is cleanliness?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, the ones who are here earlier in terms of the restaurants, they keep it clean.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But how are you working with sanitation on that issue?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We understand there won't be the underneath for the rats.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I got that, but you know, it's other things.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How how how are we gonna work on that?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, the restaurant is required to clean all the way around their setup.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer questions DOT on the fee structure for outdoor dining, and DOT provides examples comparing outdoor fees to indoor commercial rent.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: It has 105 seats.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But then again, for reference, um, every entree is over $40, and the average drink is over $20.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So I want to try to put it in perspective.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: It's by a private entity.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: There needs to be a fair charge for that space, and we think these are more than reasonable costs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My last question is of course on the upper west side, I think you're charging too much compared to other locations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I am gonna try to lower that to make it more comparable with other areas because not every restaurant is huge, not every restaurant is as big as some of the ones you just mentioned in Soho.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So that I think needs to be addressed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I will I don't know that you could comment on it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, uh Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah I I just want the open conversation because while we trying to do the right thing, which I'm very optimistic.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler challenges DOT's characterization of the program as a success, pointing to the low approval rates and the loss of thousands of outdoor dining setups.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Questions DOT on Program Success and Low Approval Rates

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much to Chairs Menon and Brooks Powers for convening this important hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Um just how would DOT qualify the permanent outdoor dining program to date?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: An abject failure, a moderate failure, a severe work in progress?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Like what's the none of the above, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We feel the program is going very well, and we look forward to having more improvements in the future.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: I mean, and again, if I can just mention we have um about almost four thousand applications, it's a very good number.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Four thousand applications.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: At the time that the open dining program season began in an un what is an unfortunate seasonal program, and I really did appreciate Chair Menon's remarks earlier about her support for a year-round program.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I certainly agree with her on that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Uh five hundred and forty-seven twenty-one roadway dining restaurants had been approved, five hundred and forty-seven had received conditional approval.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So that's what, seven percent of the height of the peak of the outdoor program.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that bureaucratic challenges from DOT?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If I recall correctly, in the negotiations over this bill, one of the mayor's office's top priorities and the commissioner's top priorities was for DOT to own this program.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This was really important to you all that you wanted to be the lead on outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Am I right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So why?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is it bureaucratic challenges and limitations that have only seven percent of restaurants receiving conditional approval at the beginning of the outdoor dining season?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So let me clarify that as we speak, 80% of the restaurants that have applied are able and are setting up as months into the vast majority of the restaurants and what we've discussed here today is that we have a number of re requirements set between the city charter and the local law that that do add to the length of the process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Let's just drill down on the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So 4,000 is the number you said applied, 80% of that 4,000 have received conditional approval.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler disputes DOT's statistics regarding the peak number of participating restaurants during the pandemic, arguing that the program has been severely diminished.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Disputes DOT's Statistics on Pandemic-Era Participation

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that am I using the right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do I have the right denominators and I can't.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we have basically um 2600, 2,603 restaurants are able to operate right now.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, so that's not 80% of 4,000.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So and I can explain, and I ran through these numbers earlier.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So 263 out of 13,000 was our peak at the height of COVID.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: No, council member.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Let me explain this.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Between our at the height, it was between six and eight thousand restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That was the end of COVID.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That was the peak?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: No, that was the peak.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And what I'll tell you about the 13,000 number, those are restaurants who may have signed up.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: They may or may not have set up.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We don't know.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: They may have gone out of business.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: That was not uh the level of the city.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But even if they went out of business, they would count.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I don't understand.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: You're saying what I'm telling you is that between six and eight thousand businesses had dining setups, so this and we have about four thousand applications to date.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So we actually have quite a good percentage of the city.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The data and that has been widely reported in just about every outlet in the city of New York that up to 13,000 establishments have participated in outdoor dining, that's inaccurate.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: What we what we explained is at the height, it was six to eight thousand.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So why is there such an extraordinary discrepancy?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This has all been under DOT's purview.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Why would only half as many?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Because 13,000 at some point may have signed up to participate in the program, but we don't think there was ever a time that 13,000 were set up at that moment.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rick Rodriguez
utterances.text: I believe the hospitality alliance also referenced the six to eight thousand number in their tests.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: As as the peak at the end, and I believe they referenced that number at the conclusion of the COVID period.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That was the number that was in effect.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It was not the end of, it was during the peak.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That's not what their testimony says, but beside the point, their testimony references the 13,000 figure that we're all familiar with that has been widely reported in the press that's been noted about.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So you're saying the 13,000 figure is inaccurate?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That never we didn't have third, or we had 13,000 restaurants over the world.

utterances.speaker: Rick Rodriguez
utterances.text: So the ad might not be the useful number for the purposes of calculating the success at this point in time.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So even still, let's use your numbers, which I would argue with, and I don't I I'm I'm not really sure at all are accurate, but we'll accept your numbers that a peak of let's say 8,000 was the total number that operated any given time.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're now at 2600, right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just accepting your numbers, that means we're at less than 30% of the peak number today.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler and Chair Julie Menin discuss the need for new legislation to revamp the outdoor dining program and potentially transfer jurisdiction to a licensing agency.
