utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Less 70% fewer restaurants are operating today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Could I just get a yes-no on that?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So 2600 are operating today as we speak.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And you said a peak was 8,000.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we're talking about 2600.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And then we have a number of others that are in the process, and we have some who have dropped out of the process or are not eligible.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: I just more to these numbers.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: You need to dig into them a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Rick Rodriguez
utterances.text: Happy to dig in with the rate of from temporary to permanent.

utterances.speaker: Rick Rodriguez
utterances.text: Um the rate uh is similar to what Paris has, which had a pre-existing program as well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Uh beside the point of whatever happened in Paris.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We know what we've done in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We know that restaurants and and bars want this in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've seen how much great a success it is in New York City, and we've seen that this administration and this legislation killed the program.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we see only a small fraction of the number of restaurants and establishments are actually operating outdoor dining today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've prioritize parking spots over having space for our bars and restaurants to be able to expand into our communities and make our streets more vibrant and dynamic, expand employment at our small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This is not working.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I am working on legislation to totally revamp outdoor dining because I think it's a failure.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I disagree with your assessment.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: My assessment is failure.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I think we really need to get to work on how to make this a success.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I just want to say I agree.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I don't think you are here for part of my opening remark where I called it an unmitigated disaster.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I'm urging and I hope, yes, and and I do hope that in because I've got a number of LS requests to revamp it, too.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I hope that you will consider this idea of transferring the program to DCWP, a licensing agency as opposed to a transit agency.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I think a lot of the problems uh come from the fact that we need a licensing agency with a walk-in licensing center.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: DCWP has historic expertise and sidewalk cafe licenses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We certainly did not have these problems when that agency was doing that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, I I just will say I during the legislative process I thought DCWP was a more logical home.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But you're welcome to continue those conversations as we go through our drafting process and would love to work with you together.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I think if DOT wants to own this program long term, we just need to see significant improvement.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I do want to ask one further question before I turn over to Councilmember Lewis.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin asks DOT if the agency sent direct communications to all 13,000 restaurants that participated in the temporary pandemic-era program.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Of the thirteen thousand one hundred and four restaurants that were just referenced, have you sent a letter to every single one of them to see if they want to apply for the new program?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And every single one has been visited.

chapters.summary: Council Member Farah Louis asks DOT about the coordination between the city and the State Liquor Authority (SLA) for businesses seeking to serve alcohol in outdoor setups.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And I just want to echo some of the sentiments of my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We appreciate the efforts of DOT.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Penny has been very resourceful, but we do believe that there's still flaws with this program.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So we're going to continue to echo that and mention it, but we do hope for change, especially change in agency.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: However, the Department of Transportation has not clearly articulated what is required for these businesses to lawfully operate under the state liquor authority.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So I wanted to ask what coordination, if any, is currently in place between DOT and SLA to inform businesses of their obligations when they want to modify their operations of outdoor alcohol service.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So I wanted to know what ongoing interagency communication mechanisms exist to ensure small businesses are not receiving conflicting or incomplete information from multiple city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So one is on health and safety standards, the other one is uh inter-agency coordination.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay, I'll answer the second one.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Michelle won't answer the first one.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So as far as DOHMH, Michelle mentioned that we do have quarterly meetings with the agencies to make sure that we are coordinating carefully.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um we haven't heard that restaurants are confused about the DOHMH guidelines of serving outdoors, but if there's any more to that, um, and we'll go back and we'll double check with the staff, or if you have any more information, if you let us know, we'll make sure we clear up any confusion because we don't want them to be confused between the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: In terms of the state liquor authority, we've had a number of conversations with them.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: They've actually been very collaborative, um, and they are recognizing the conditional approvals we've issued for roadway cafes as sufficient approval to move forward with the modification to the liquor license that restaurants need to serve alcohol outside.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right, so what specific efforts is DOT undertaking to ensure businesses do not fall in non-compliance due to this lack of coordination, if any?

chapters.summary: Council Member Farah Louis asks DOT about interagency communication mechanisms to ensure small businesses do not receive conflicting information regarding health and safety standards.
chapters.title: Council Member Louis Questions DOT on Interagency Health and Safety Standards

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So, how do you make sure that they're compliant?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Yeah, well, we're we're happy to make sure, and as Michelle said, we have an excellent relationship with State Liquor throughout this process, it's been some unchartered territory, they've worked well with us.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: If there's anyone falling through the cracks, we are happy to reach out to the State Laker Authority.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And you will make sure there's some cross-coordination there to make sure that the business we have been coordinating very closely with them.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We speak with them all the time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then Councilmember Rivera.

chapters.summary: Council Member Carlina Rivera questions DOT on the high costs of compliant setups available in the online marketplace and how the city can support sustainable investments.
chapters.title: Council Member Rivera Questions DOT on Marketplace Costs and Compliant Setups

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Um, thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: I want to thank everyone who testified.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Really appreciate your work and um all the small businesses that are here.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Um this is the first year of outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: I appreciate that you're evaluating the timeline for review and especially considering our own capacity and also the capacity of the community board.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: They're they're very worried about handling the applications and ensuring there's this balance, as you all know, with this with our own local ecosystems.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: So let me ask about the online marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Um, they provide elements for fabrication.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Some of them are very, very expensive, um, as you might have heard, requiring tens of thousands of dollars in deposits.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Does DOT have any information they can share about usage and feedback you've received on what's available?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Yes, we have heard anecdotally, council member, that businesses have found the marketplace to be very useful.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We don't require them to tell us if they've used it or not, because things are so busy we don't want to put anything else onto them.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So I can't really tell you exactly how many have partaken in the different aspects of the marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: But my understanding is that we have, you know, a variety of um levels of materials, you know, from high-end to more everyday materials.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: So in the marketplace, there should be enough for them to choose from to be able to fit their budget.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Well, you can imagine they want to make an investment, they want something to last.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Um, so when we're looking at I've I've seen price tags 35,000, 86,000 for pieces.

chapters.summary: Council Member Carlina Rivera asks DOT if vestibules and winterizing enclosures are permitted under the permanent sidewalk cafe guidelines.
chapters.title: Council Member Rivera Questions DOT on Vestibules and Winterizing Sidewalk Cafes

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: I mean, that's a that's a lot for business to take on.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: So I know you've received a lot of feedback on that.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: So we would love to work with you on figuring out how we can make things a little bit more sustainable.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Um in the winter time, sidewalk cafes have these awnings they'll enclose that which is called a vestibule.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Is that in line with the program?

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Is that something that is included in the program?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um it is not.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: The vestibule is just a small enclosure that prevents like the wind and the cold air from getting in.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: No, that is not part of the program.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Well, we just, you know, there are so many things that make the experience um that's important to the experience at at these businesses.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: So I wanted to ask because a lot of uh operators are interested in that being considered.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: This also has to do with uh I guess clearance.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: You said 1,700 applications are pending, correct?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Um no.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Well, we have applications at different stages in the process.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: Okay, so we have about 800 that are in um discussions with the restaurants to keep to finalize them and move them along.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: I know you're you're speaking to some where their applications are are pending but they are operating, and they've been told that they must comply with the new clearances.

chapters.summary: Council Member Carlina Rivera questions DOT on the number of pending applications and whether the agency has the capacity to scale up to handle higher volumes.
chapters.title: Council Member Rivera Questions DOT on Pending Applications and Scaling Capacity

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: And so it's it's been a little bit difficult to ask that they be in compliance if their plans have technically not been reviewed and approved yet.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: So, how are you dealing with with that description?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: And we are working with those restaurants, so the clearances should be um very clear to them about what they need to do, but being they haven't completely gone through the process, we will we will correct them, we will notify them if we see an issue until such time they've gotten through the whole process.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Um I appreciate the time.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: I I don't want to go over, I just want to say that I know the number 8,000 was brought up as during the pandemic, how many operators there were.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Do you think that DOT or that the administration can scale up to handle a number that is closer to 8,000 rather than the three to four thousand that we're saying?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgioni
utterances.text: We would like to see the number continue to grow and we can handle it.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Okay, I appreciate that because I I'm I'm sympathetic to the demands on your agency.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: We want to be fair to residents and businesses, and I know the businesses want to be celebrated by their neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: And I think year-round, I second all the things the chair said um would be really really instrumental to the program.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Carlina Rivera
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much for your testimony today.

chapters.summary: Chair Julie Menin outlines the rules of decorum and time limits for public testimony, inviting the first panel of public witnesses to come forward.
chapters.title: Transition to Public Testimony and Decorum Rules

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Uh, we appreciate it, and we're now gonna move into the public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I'm now gonna open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I want to remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that decorum should be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but they may submit transcripts of such recordings to the sergeant at arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: When recognized, you will have two minutes to speak on today's hearing topic, which is of course dining out NYC program and introduction 857.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony that you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at counsel.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I'm now going to call the first panel.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: If you could please come forward.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Valerie De La Rosa, Chair of Manhattan Community Board 5, testifies on the board's review of over 300 applications, highlighting concerns about emergency travel widths, ADA compliance, and the need for robust enforcement.
chapters.title: Valerie De La Rosa Testimony: Manhattan Community Board 5

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Augustine
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Augustine
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Force.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Augustine
utterances.text: How afternoon?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Good morning, Chairs Brooks, Powers, and Minen, and members of the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure and the Committee on Consumer and Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: I am Valerie Delarosa, Chair of Manhattan Community Board 2, and I'm speaking on behalf of the board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: CB2 has held public hearings for 308 roadway and sidewalk cafe applications to date, and we're hearing 36 more applications this evening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: So far, 40% of roadway cafe applications in CB2 do not meet the required 15-foot emergency travel lane, with 99 roadway cafe applications in C B2 referred to DOT, referred by DOT to FDNY for travel lane waivers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: FDNY is reviewing those on those roadway setups on a case-by-case basis with no agency overseeing overall block by block by block analysis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: We have as many as 10 roadway setups on narrow streets for this waiver that do not meet the on one block that do not meet the waiver, uh the 15-foot travel lane.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Outdoor dining has been a resounding success in CB2.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Revocable consent fees are 55% cheaper than before COVID for most of CB2.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: CB2 has 564 roadway cafe and sidewalk cafe applications expected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: That is 23% of outdoor dining cafes in Manhattan and 30 or 30 23% in all five boroughs and 36% in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: We've revert reviewed 60% of those.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: We've had the most sidewalk cafes before COVID, during COVID, and now during dining out NYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: The one size fits all program doesn't meet the needs of our mixed use and residential communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: A bar with 80 seats outside has 53 more seats outside than inside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Hotels with roadway dining in their loading zones, and now all of their hotel loading and unloading occurs in the street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Primary building entrances of five feet are not met.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Sidewalk cafe applications that propose non-ADA compliant cafe widths of one foot nine inches not only is unworkable, but it's illegal under federal ADA law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: FDA ADA requirements are hidden on a 311 knowledge page for outdoor dining complaints related to accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: And recently, NYC DOT removed a bullet point requiring a 36-inch accessible route within outdoor dining seating areas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Again, this is not an NYC DOT guideline, it is federal ADA law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: What's working?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Seasonal roadway dining works in Manhattan Community Board 2.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Seasonal roadway dining is what we want in our neighborhoods, not seasonal storage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: They allow the open air dining opens up the public realm, and the dining out NYC legislation ensures that sidewalk cafes remain open air.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: The clear path requirements are based on the mobility plan and they are sound and they provide the protections.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Before making any additional changes to this program, we strongly urge you to look into the enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Valerie De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Hi, um, Chair Brooks Powers and Chairman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um thank you for holding this hearing today.

chapters.summary: Lacey Tauber, representing Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso, testifies in support of restoring a year-round roadway dining program and reducing bureaucratic and cost burdens on small businesses.
chapters.title: Lacey Tauber Testimony: Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um, my name is Lacey Tower.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: I'm representing Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um he was here, but unfortunately he had to leave.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um, this is a really big uh priority for the borough president because he was the uh the author of the bill that created the um emergency open restaurants program and a precursor to the bill that the council ultimately passed in 2023 to make the program permanent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um BP Reynoso is very proud of what the original open restaurants program accomplished.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: It was a lifeline for small restaurants that struggled during the pandemic, saved approximately 100,000 jobs, allowed for people to interact socially in a safe setting, generated tax revenue for the city, and helped New Yorkers reimagine what our streets can be.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: It was wildly successful by any objective means, and it's a shame to see that because of the changes that the council adopted, the program now barely exists, with only about a third of restaurants that had approved emergency permits choosing to apply for the quote unquote permanent program, and barely a handful of those applications approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: It's up to the council to now fix some of the mistakes and some of the recommendations that the BP has are first to restore a year-round outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: The seasonal program creates too many burdens on small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: I'm sure that has been discussed and will be discussed more today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: It would also encourage more creative designs, restore a consistent option for immunocompromised people to socialize safely and create more jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Two to expedite approvals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: It is unacceptable that DOT had processed and approved less than 1% of applications as of mid-February, and even allowing businesses with completed applications to proceed doesn't involve uh doesn't solve issues with SLA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Another important way to speed the process up is to remove the oversight role for community boards.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Uh, to the extent allowed under the charter, they should have little to no role in the approval process as the agency level requirements are sufficient to ensure safety, appropriateness, cleanliness, and accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um, finally, alleviate cost burdens for small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um, this was a big goal for BP in the original legislation to make it easy as possible for small outer borough and immigrant-run businesses to participate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: That was the point of updating the zoning text amendment as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um, and uh, I will just list these to remove the public hearing notice fee, ensure that the application requirements are clear, create a process for expansion in front of adjacent businesses, and remove the liquor liability requirement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Um, thank you again for holding this hearing for President Reno looks forward to working with the council to make the program successful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lacey Tauber
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Jean Ryan, I'm president of Disabled in Action of Metropolitan New York, DIA for short.

chapters.summary: Jean Ryan, President of Disabled in Action of Metropolitan NY, advocates strongly for ADA accessibility in outdoor dining setups, opposing year-round sheds that block sidewalks and create physical barriers.
chapters.title: Jean Ryan Testimony: Disabled in Action of Metropolitan NY

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: We are in favor of outdoor dining, and we do not want to go back to the year-round sheds which were mostly inaccessible to people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: And for the record, the first time I heard anybody today talking about accessibility were from these two people at the table.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Nobody here mentioned it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: No council member and no restaurant person, and no DOT person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Some of us enjoy outdoor dining and like to eat out with our friends and families, but if an outdoor restaurant is not accessible to people with disabilities because it has a step up or down, like what typically happened in the emergency ones, or has a steep ramp or the tables are too close together, restaurants do not get our business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: This is 2025, 35 years after the ADA was enacted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: You'd think the restaurant executives would want accessibility because it's good for business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: In the 2020 version of sheds, no amount of complaints by us, even by the mayor's office for people with disabilities, MOBED, made any difference.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: We definitely do not want to go back to those bad old days.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: And that was when the other agency was in charge, and they did nothing about accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: So people are saying you want to go back to that agency, and they did nothing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: You know, that doesn't make any sense to us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: I spent much time with architects working on what eventually became the new prototypes for restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: We are in favor of the new prototypes, but we are extremely concerned about the one prototype that allows for a ramp, because if there is no ramp available, then that outdoor restaurant is totally inaccessible to us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: I voiced this concern during the early discussions, but DOT wanted to help more restaurants be able to be outdoors if the landscape was not level enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: I need more time, I can't read well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: It might be hip to have high stools and chairs and high tables, but they are inaccessible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Then we can't even go to that place.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: As the years went on, many sheds were eyesores, and people are not really talking about that today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: And many were not the economic engine that they were purported to be because of lack of staffed wait on tables outside and inside, and the sheds smelled like rotten food that was rat fodder.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: They were not hygienic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Many sheds were abandoned or used for storage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: I saw Christmas trees and construction materials in the sheds, as well as stack tables and chairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Streets are for everyone, not just for restaurants to have a structure on them year in and year out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: And nothing, some were nice, but most were shabby and unkempt, and we're not paid painted or kept up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: So we have to give everyone the same amount of time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm gonna ask if you could please wrap up and then you can submit the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Yes, so I just say that we want accessibility, and that has to be a priority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Ryan
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm gonna call the next panel.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Kathleen Irwin, Sarah Lynd, Cecil Brooks Jr., if you could please come down, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Yes, I see you are immediately revision.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: What?

utterances.speaker: Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Hello, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: My name is Kathleen Irwin with the New York State Restaurant Association.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Um, thank you for holding this hearing today and giving us the opportunity to discuss the rollout of Dining Out NYC.

chapters.summary: Kathleen Irwin, representing the New York State Restaurant Association, acknowledges DOT's educational resources but highlights significant challenges with application backlogs, seasonality, and high costs.
chapters.title: Kathleen Irwin Testimony: New York State Restaurant Association

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Um, I want to begin by saying I recognize that the institution of a permanent program is a major undertaking, and I want to start by acknowledging some of the things that DOT has done well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Um, first and foremost, they've been willing to educate and engage with restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: They've provided extensive visual resources online, trainings both in person and online, and have partnered with SBS to promote the SBS financial estimator tool on their website.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Um, that being said, in the rollout, we've seen a number of challenges that have made it both less attractive for restaurants to apply and difficult for those who did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: One of those is that the timing and incentive structure of the application program caused a major backlog, which created an inability for restaurants to plan and invest while waiting to find out if they're approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Limited and delayed coordination with other agencies, including the SLA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: I know we just heard otherwise, but that's not the experience that we have heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: The application process overall being so difficult that many restaurants have been forced to rely on private expeditors to feel confident in their applications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: The seasonality of roadway dining creating a major cost and logistical burden for restaurants who are expected to build, maintain, deconstruct, and store the same set of materials year in and year out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Finally, inflexible siting with that has limited the ability for cafes to be as they once were, both during open restaurants and even during the sidewalk cafe program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: One example that was mentioned earlier in terms of timeline mismanagement is the fact that the creation of conditional approvals wasn't even announced until February 28th, with approvals beginning to be mailed March 3rd for a March 25th start to build date.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: That's three weeks or less for restaurants to find out if they're even allowed to make a major operational and capital investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: And it's not acceptable given the amount of foresight that we all had going into this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Moving forward, we have a few short and long-term recommendations for this program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: We expect that the backlog problem will begin to resolve with higher application volumes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: But besides that, we would like to see year-round roadway cafes before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You can say did you want to finish the sentence?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: I have like five recommendations, but.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: If you could um submit it and we will look at that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And we have to under our rules give everyone the same amount of time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Hello, Chris.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Oh, I'm not sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Thank you for everyone and the um the opportunity to present on behalf of Open Plans and Happy National Picnic Day.

chapters.summary: Cecil, representing Open Plans, discusses research on public space activation, recommending year-round roadway dining and greater flexibility for small businesses with limited storefront frontage.
chapters.title: Cecil Testimony: Open Plans

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: So, in honor of all of the wonderful eating spaces that we have in our city and the foodie capital of the world, I would like to share just a little bit anecdotally about some of the research that we've done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: We had the opportunity to speak with dozens of businesses and visit hundreds and thousands of square feet of space to hear what has been among the pressing challenges of businesses, entrepreneurs, and pop-up shops.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Everyone who has been looking to use outdoor space in a creative way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: And uh especially as someone who started his career at SBS working on the sidewalk cafe policies with with um many folks in this room.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: We find that a year-round option is among the simplest, most straightforward um recommendations that that can be taken into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: And also, yes, the agency does matter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Government and the policymakers are the ones who will be working to roll up their sleeves and put this um in action.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Also, having smaller businesses account for their size and using adjacent uh store frontage when they don't have the uh structure or the uh size to really make use of of their um eating space because I can't tell you how many places, especially in the outer boroughs where there isn't an immediate grid, have less than a thousand or even five hundred feet of space um with none of their neighbors doing anything in front of their storefronts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: So having some flexibility and how businesses use their storefront space, that would be another immediate um improvement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: And also transparency with the fees and the expenses that come up after the application process, with what materials are required and who and what and where businesses can get their materials from.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: These are all things that would make it much more accessible to have a space be activated in front of their storefront.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cecil Brooks Jr.
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sara Lind, Co-Executive Director of Open Plans, urges the Council to amend the legislation to allow year-round roadway dining, noting that seasonal storage and deconstruction costs are prohibitive for small businesses.
chapters.title: Sara Lind Testimony: Open Plans

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Outdoor dining is a vibrant use of public space that New Yorkers and restaurants love, and we were excited to see the program codified, but we have serious concerns about flaws in the present program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: You've heard over and over again about the lack of a year-round option, which has drastically lowered participation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: As you've heard, thousands in additional costs to take down store and set up structures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Restaurants don't have the space to store materials over the winter months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: I think you know, having this permanent program and the way that restaurants are now really opting in and intentionally participating.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: I think we will see the restaurants that choose to use an outdoor dining option year-round that will be much more intentional about that with the new uh design uh regulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Um, we expect to see uh those concerns um addressed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Furthermore, there's no logical reason why we would allow sidewalk cafes year-round, but not roadway dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Um, New Yorkers want to eat outside on a nice day any time of the year, whether it be on the sidewalk or in the roadway.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Um, and many New Yorkers, speaking of accessibility, are still dealing with health issues that they want to be able to eat outside and not inside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: So we encourage the council to amend the legislation to provide a year-round option.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Cecil just mentioned the existing frontage regulations, which limit participation from restaurants with smaller storefronts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Again, during the temporary program, restaurants were allowed to make agreements with neighboring businesses to extend the footprint of their outdoor dining structure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: For small restaurants, it just doesn't make sense right now unless they can do that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: Finally, we believe the current program incentivizes sidewalk cafes over roadway cafes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: And while sidewalk cafes are an important part of the vibrant public realm, they do create challenges on crowded sidewalks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: And many restaurants also don't even have a wide enough sidewalk in front to participate in the sidewalk cafe program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sarah Lind
utterances.text: So the city needs to make these amendments to the program to ensure that the roadway cafe program is just as successful as the sidewalk program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Yes, please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: They're brief.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: The first is year-round roadway cafes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: We believe that that will make the program more accessible, affordable, and give businesses the ability to invest in beautiful creative structures that are high quality and include the higher materials and building standards that have been part of the dining out NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: An example of that is the water-filled barriers that are not the rat burrows that the sand and dirt barriers were in the temporary program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: We would like to see more assistance from the city so that restaurants don't feel the need to hire private expediters to feel confident in their application.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: We would like to see more flexibility in sighting and the availability of waivers for those that had sidewalk or roadway cafes so located during open restaurants or in the prior sidewalk cafe program with no safety issues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: We would like to track the dining out dining out NYC usage by neighborhood with an eye to increase participation in areas with low participation rates, and that assistance could include things like targeted technical support, waived application fees, or upfront grants to help mitigate the upfront costs of starting with dining out NYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Finally, we would love to see the city identifying neighborhoods where dining out NYC is going well, that would be well suited to increase pedestrianization to create dining out NYC districts that combine additional open space with formal encouragement and promotion of dining out NYC to be a draw for locals and tourists alike.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Irwin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you to the panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We'll now hear from Charlotta Jansen, Kevin, Mulligan, Dior St.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Hilaire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: So I'm Charlotta Jansen of Shea Oscar and Bedsty.

chapters.summary: Charlotta Janssen, owner of Chez Oskar in Bed-Stuy, describes the severe financial and operational toll of complying with the new rules, calling the regulations anti-business and anti-community.
chapters.title: Charlotta Janssen Testimony: Chez Oskar

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I applied in July.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I am compliant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I still have not received a license, and it's been hell.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Let's be real.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: The current DOT rules are killing restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Follow them, bleed out, break them, get killed by fines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: This March DOT came down hard on me with four summonses in under two weeks for my canopy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: All winter I had begged LPC to review my design.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: There's no precedence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I'm tossed around between LPC, DOB, and DOT again and again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I had to cut back my canopy, which served my community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I had to cut them back to be awnings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I am compliant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: But what I'm left with is a setup that doesn't serve my customers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: It doesn't serve my block, and it sure as hell doesn't serve the spirit of outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I can only cover half my diners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: I can't enclose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Try planning service and food outdoors every day.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: If it rains, if temperatures dip, it's over.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Imagine doing that at a wedding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: During the pandemic, canopies didn't just help, they saved us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Shea Oscars, Secret Garden, Zaka Cafe, DSKL, La National.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: We made it because we had beautiful, strong, enclosable and sound mitigating canopies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: They gave us real capacity, real shelter, and real presence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: We created solutions because we were allowed to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Now we're being punished for that creativity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: The new rules are anti business, anti design, and anti community, based on the worst actors and enforced like we are criminals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: This isn't regulation, it's sabotage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Please allow for enclosable canopies on the sidewalk and roadside that can attach, not penetrate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Allow for full coverage of diners and where needed pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Allow for self-certification, unless there's a history of serious violations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Make roadside year round, require removal when roads are repaved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: No need for landlord consent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Um small bomb and pop businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: We're not asked I'm closing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: We're not asking for chaos.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: We're asking for the freedom to build what saved us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Stop choking the very engine that kept this city eating.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Let outdoor dining remain a space for creativity and community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Charlotta Janssen
utterances.text: Let us keep building what worked instead of forgettable feeding troughs.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Kevin Mulligan, owner of The Laurels in the East Village, criticizes the ban on enclosed sidewalk cafes, explaining the logistical impossibility of storing large amounts of furniture overnight.
chapters.title: Kevin Mulligan Testimony: The Laurels

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Hey everyone, my name is Kevin Mulligan, and I own a bar restaurant called the Laurels in the East Village.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: We are almost two years old, and I hope to see three.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: The one egregious major flaw with the new rules is it forbids the use of enclosed sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: This is nonsensical and unfair for a number of reasons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: The city is granting licenses for year-round dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: This is a very disingenuous offer as it completely ignores the fact that for at least six to seven months of the year over fall, winter, and spring, no one can or wants to sit outside due to the elements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Businesses are being asked to pay for a 12-month license when any reasonable person can see it is not usable for a significant portion of that time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: We are well into April now, and for much of the first few weeks, a lot of days barely made it into the 50s, and that's during daytime hours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: By peak dinner hours, it was too cold to sit outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: So from October to April, outdoor dining under the new guidelines is essentially redundant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Secondly, storage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Inherently, outdoor dining means there is obviously an amount of tables and chairs placed outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: There are also barriers placed around them to demarcate the area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Where are businesses expected to store all this at night?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: In my own case, given the extreme breadth of the sidewalk on my corner of 2nd Avenue, there could be 45 to 55 tables outside and 90 to 110 chairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: I cannot move the furniture inside after the 10 or 11 p.m.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: curfew on using the outdoor area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: There will be customers inside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Like most places, I don't have enough basement storage.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: And even if I did, it is not feasible nor safe to ask the one member of staff who opens every day to lug all that furniture up the steps from the basement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: The only solution left to me is to stack mountains of furniture outside on the pavement every night.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: This leads to numerous other issues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: It is unsightly, it gives a very strong impression my business is closed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: It is difficult to tie all this down to prevent it from being stolen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: The area will likely be used as a toilet by some.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: This furniture is going to fall into disrepair very quickly from having to sit outside the elements every night.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kevin Mulligan
utterances.text: Just to close, um, if the issue is not the space the business is taking up on the pavement, and it is not, as the city has conceded this by permitting outdoor dining at all, then what significant reason can there be to disallow the business from keeping its customers warm and dry during colder months or just rainy days in general?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for the panel.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next we'll hear from Mary Inbancho, Bonasio Crespi, Augustine Holt, and Leslie Clark.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: It's on.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: You can begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Mary Avancho, resident of New York City for 46 years.

chapters.summary: Mary Honcho, a 46-year resident of NYC, raises concerns about the quality of life impacts of outdoor dining, including garbage accumulation, kitchen capacity limits, and bathroom availability.
chapters.title: Mary Honcho Testimony: Resident Perspective

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: Our city is suffering, and as Andrew Ridgie stated at a community board meeting, New York City has an enforcement problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: I agree with Andrew, and will add that in that enforcement problem pertains to outdoor dining as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: I love the city and want to see it thrive, but let's be clear: no other private industry has been afforded this opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: It is my opinion there are too many unanswered questions to decide this is a year-round program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: Let us first determine how well it works, how it can live up to expect expectations for both the industry and New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: When doubling the size of an establishment, where is that garbage placed if setups extend full frontage?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: It sits in front of adjacent businesses and residences, an issue that is still not being addressed on applications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: How does a restaurant double its size and continue to operate out of the same-size kitchen?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: Is this realistic?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: How does it double its customers and accommodate them with the same number of bathrooms?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: I encourage the hospitality industry to be patient, realistic, and not greedy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: To communicate to applicants that they abide by guidelines and respect their neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: This will be your pathway to success.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: This is how we together will properly grow our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Avanzato
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: My name is Augustine Hope.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: I'm from the West Village Residents Association, a community that has been disproportionately impacted by this program.

chapters.summary: Augustine Hope, representing the West Village Residents Association, testifies about the disproportionate negative impact of roadway dining setups on residential quality of life, citing noise and trash.
chapters.title: Augustine Hope Testimony: West Village Residents Association

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: I had prepared a long testimony, but most of the committee have left, so let me just address a couple of salient points.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: The hospitality industry has driven a truck through an illusion that they have made about this program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: That it that the outdoor dining is what is a monolithic program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: It isn't.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: With them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: So perhaps we could have a hearing that doesn't focus on all the problems that the hospitality industry is having.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: And we're happy to consult with them on how to comply with the rules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: They're not that complicated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: We'll do it for free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: We won't charge them anything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: So perhaps we could have a hearing at some point where we discuss the issues that the community is having and some of the ways that they could actually be solved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: They're not that complicated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: I th I there's no time here now to discuss them, but let's talk about them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Augustine Hope
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Leslie Clark, representing the West Village Residents Association, criticizes the reduction of pedestrian clearances on sidewalks and praises the pre-pandemic enforcement model of the DCWP.
chapters.title: Leslie Clark Testimony: West Village Residents Association

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: Leslie, I'm Leslie Clark, also with West Village Residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: I want you to talk about some of the suggestions that have been made by the by the industry that I disagree with, and that has been made by some council members that I disagree with.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: One is about the clearances.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: First, I'd like to make it very clear that instead of using the word clearances, let's talk about the space allotted to pedestrians on a public sidewalk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: That's what these clearances are about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: Restaurants consider these onerous.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: What is onerous is that under the old, and by the way, I agree with you, Councilmember Menon, excellently run program by the uh consumer affairs department.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: We had a three-foot pedestrian clearance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: We had a three-foot um service aisle in front of every uh sidewalk cafe, in addition to an eight-foot pedestrian clearance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: And by the way, we also had excellent enforcement by consumer affairs, because unlike the complaint-driven system that DOT has, consumer affairs actually sent out inspectors to look for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: And as a result, what we had was a uniform compliance and uniform enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: Every restaurant knew it had to follow the rules because the next door neighbor followed the rules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: And why did they follow the rules?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Leslie Clark
utterances.text: Because consumer affairs took it upon themselves to make sure they were following the rules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney
utterances.text: The Dining Out program offers a vibrant opportunity for the community growth and engagement, but it has to do so while ensuring disability access to these restaurants and the sidewalks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney
utterances.text: Um, sidewalk access is paramount for the disability community, um, especially considering that a lot of other transportation options like the subway that millions of New Yorkers rely on is not accessible, which often leaves the sidewalk as the primary mode of navigation for everyday life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney
utterances.text: Um, when sidewalks are blocked either by um dining structures or by server and patron traffic, people with disabilities often have to navigate into the street, which is incredibly dangerous, not just for um themselves, but for others.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney
utterances.text: Um, luckily, dining out mandates a clear path for sidewalks and other accessibility requirements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Madison Pinkney
utterances.text: Um, additionally, the dining out program should offer an annual report of these accessibility complaints and the city's responses, which will help ensure accountability not just for restaurants, but also for the city is enforcement.

chapters.summary: John Grimes, a 35-year resident of the West Village, testifies via Zoom to oppose any loosening of outdoor dining rules, citing noise, drunken behavior, and the lack of an environmental impact study.
chapters.title: John Grimes Testimony: West Village Resident

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Hello, uh, I'm John Grimes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Uh, can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm the first one to do that.
