utterances.text: Yes, we can.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Great, great, thanks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Uh, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: I am a 35-year resident of the West Village, and I have experienced the impact of the outdoor dining program firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Um, I actually agree with some of the comments that have been made about the program being an unmitigated disaster, but that is because in my view, the underlying premise of the program is critically flawed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: While necessary during a pandemic, the pandemic is now over.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: What we have now remaining is an illegal giveaway of public land to the hospitality industry with very little payments, notwithstanding the comments on that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: And without having done an environmental impact study that would take into account the impact on the environment, including the ambience environments, noise.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: No one has talked about the environment here, really, which is astounding to me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Uh I am opposed to any change to the program that would loosen the rules or provide for year-round outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: From my personal experience, my quality of life has been severely harmed by this program and the associated noise and drunken behavior from the establishment of my immediate neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: I live in a historic district and I'm required to keep my property looking as it did basically in the 19th century under the landmark laws, which I'm happy to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: In contrast, the sheds are mostly eyesores, and in my neighborhood are used for outdoor drinking, not outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: I heard some comments about limited seating, but seating is irrelevant if patrons are standing around and drinking, spilling out into the sidewalk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: These sheds are not a solution for sidewalk crowding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Instead, they contribute to it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: I have made personally over a hundred complaints to 311 of the New York State Liquor Authority with no relief.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Party goes on outside my house, and until recently, year-round.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: I heard some interesting comments about the rules being based on bad actors, but in my experience, bad actors are allowed to proliferate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: I also heard a lot of complaints about the new roadway setups that are supposed to be removable, but as the sheds are now going back up, we see they're the same enclosed structure, which, if a lot of proliferate year-round, your time has expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: And I oppose any loosening of the current rules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - John Grimes
utterances.text: And please.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Next speaker, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Hi, uh, my name is Kiana.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: I live on the Lower East Side, and I'm testifying today in favor of all outdoor dining accommodations, including sidewalk seating, roadside dining, and open streets, which are some of the only ways immunocompromised New Yorkers and other folks at high risk of complications from COVID and the flu can safely enjoy New York's restaurants.

chapters.summary: Kiara Sakiheld, a Lower East Side resident, testifies via Zoom in favor of outdoor dining as a crucial public health accommodation for immunocompromised individuals.
chapters.title: Kiara Sakiheld Testimony: Lower East Side Resident

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: The subtext of the opposition to outdoor dining is that it was a COVID era program, but because COVID is over, the program should be curtailed and our streets should be returned to cars.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: But COVID is still very real for many New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: And if you're concerned about ADA violations, please remember that disability can take many forms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: And I'm very concerned that being high risk or immunocompromised has been entirely overlooked in this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: It's not lost on me that the city's own rules for in-person testimony today include COVID safety protocols like masking that don't seem to be widely adopted or enforced, which serves as a visible reminder for how vulnerable people are further excluded from public life.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Vanishing outdoor dining options are particularly troubling when Kathy Hochel is pushing for a statewide mask ban.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Vaccine uptake is terrible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: COVID cases surged last summer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: The CDC is cutting billions in funds to help state to health departments respond to COVID.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: And Gotham has recently reported that 500,000 New Yorkers have long COVID, which can be so debilitating it prevents them from being able to work and support themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Every new COVID case is a new opportunity for one of our neighbors to get seriously ill, die, or experience long-term complications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Outdoor dining also plays a role in stopping the spread of flu, and this year flu cases were at their highest level since 2020.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: There have been 23 pediatric deaths statewide this season, nine of which were here in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Proper ventilation is crucial to preventing airborne illness, but the city has not invested in the infrastructure needed to make indoor dining safer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: And most outdoor dining options aren't even available year-round, meaning that if you're vulnerable, you can't safely or easily participate in a huge piece of public and social life for half the year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: With all of this in mind, I'm asking the council to please make applying for roadside and sidewalk seating less cumbersome, allow year-round streeteries, and provide more flexibility for sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Outdoor dining is a public good, particularly when it comes to public health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kiana
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Next speaker, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Michelle Campo, are you able to testify?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: I'm uh testifying on behalf of Broadway Residence Coalition in Soho.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: Um I testified earlier this morning at the competing public hearing regard to a change of rules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: Um, but here, um, first of all, I would like to applaud Community Board 2 and the effort that they have done uh through their outdoor dining working group to catalog and document uh the way that uh various sidewalk cafes and street roadway setups are being uh applied for to come into community board two tonight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: Uh as Valerie De La Rosa mentioned, there will be another three dozen uh applications being heard, one of which is for a current operation with four seats, four, one, two, three, four, applying for sidewalk and roadway for an additional sixty seats outdoors, six zero.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: This is an operation without a bathroom uh because it didn't require one with only four seats, but now with 60 seats, what does that mean in terms of sharing public space?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: Uh one thing that the advocates, the paid lobbyists and lawyers for the restaurant industry didn't mention were residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: There is an inherent conflict in the uh much broader allowance for outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Peter Davies
utterances.text: I'm not against outdoor dining, but do not acknowledge the conflicts uh to act as if there have been no changes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: I'm a resident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: Uh, Peter references us as references, uh, as residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: We uh of 80 of New York City for 80 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: I strongly oppose hospitality alliance's proposal to make outdoor dining permanent and year-round on our public streets and sidewalks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: Public space is not a perk for paying customers, it's a shared right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: They are a lobbying group for private and corporate interests, and their proposal is exclusion dressed as inclusion and deregulation disguised as reform.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: This is not about supporting small business, it is about the permanent privatization of public space.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: Sidewalks and roadways belong to all New Yorkers, including residents like me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: Turning them into commercial real estate violates the public trust doctrine and undermines accessibility, mobility, and equity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: It is not our responsibility to be de facto investors in their business success and to ensure that they quote break even.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: The alliance wants to reduce clearances and expand year-round operations, but the existing program already showed us the consequences of over five years, blocked sidewalks, rodent infestations, harmful oppressive noise entering into our homes after long days' work, and serious violations of the Americans with Disabilities Act.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: These dying sheds turned the city into an obstacle course, impossible to navigate safely for wheelchair users, seniors, or parents with strollers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: And let's not forget the Department of Transportation, which is tasked with the oversight, has failed to steward this program responsibly since its inception.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: They are the alleged inspectors, yet a force enforcement was lax.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sandy Reiburn
utterances.text: Commun complaints were unanswered, and non time has expired to continue to proliferate.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next we'll hear from Elizabeth Adams, followed by Michael Cooper Smith, followed by Samir Lavinga Lavingia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Hi, good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: I'm Elizabeth Adams with Transportation Alternatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: The Dining Out NYC program is at a crisis point.

chapters.summary: Elizabeth Adams, representing Transportation Alternatives, testifies via Zoom that the permanent program is at a crisis point due to low application rates and equity issues caused by seasonal restrictions.
chapters.title: Elizabeth Adams Testimony: Transportation Alternatives

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: This was a bright spot during the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: But this year so far, only 3,400 restaurants even bothered to apply, and just 60 permits have been approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: This is nothing short of shocking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: New Yorkers love outdoor dining, from sidewalk cafes to eating on rooftops and parks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: It is a New York City staple.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: It is what makes us who we are, and why the open restaurants program was instantly popular.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: The benefits out of outdoor dining are clear.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Using a parking space for dining can raise revenue by 75%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: And open streets with outdoor dining have more businesses on the street now than before the pandemic because they make our neighborhoods more vibrant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Outdoor dining has created thousands of jobs, benefited small businesses, and made our streets lively, active, and engaging.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: But because of challenges like seasonal restrictions and lack of investment, businesses are not applying and they're not being approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: The chairs raised the issue of equity earlier that I wanted to speak to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: And the truth is that during the pandemic, outdoor dining was actually more equitable than it is today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Open restaurants brought outdoor seating to 17 neighborhoods that did not have any pre-COVID dining, and it allowed communities of color to double their share of establishments to 41% from 20%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: But that is far what is happening, far from what is happening today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: And a major reason is that the program is now seasonal, which creates huge costs and unrealistic expectations for businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: It is prohibitive to smaller restaurants who often don't have the resources to build new structures each year or so materials during the off months, which you heard about from restaurant owners earlier.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: And the result is what we expected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: The program is diminished, and the concern is that outdoor dining will now be concentrated in wealthier neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: I have to say to DOT earlier saying that this program is working well and everything's fine.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Everyone walking around, anyone walking around the city knows that that is simply not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Almost all the outdoor dining restaurants, all the outdoor restaurants in my neighborhood are gone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: They've shut down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Uh and that's the case.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: So your time has expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: We should be doing what we can to return public space to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Thank you, Elizabeth.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Have you heard that the COVID pandemic is over?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Um, I think what you've heard earlier is that people still face a number of health issues across our city, and that uh having outdoor dining is a great way that people can uh protect their health and their well-being.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Well, I agree with you that it has the ambiance that New York needs, however, the intrusion upon our streets is something that we do not need.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Okay, we acute we actually approved outdoor dining uh in a very reasonable sense, seasonal and on our streets and cafe tables so long or on our uh sidewalks, so long as it does not impede upon pedestrians walking.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Uh the outdoor outdoor dining sheds were a life preserver for many during COVID pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Nobody will argue that fact with you.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: However, the fact that they are an intrusion upon parking spaces and they're actually hurting small businesses by doing so.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: There have been alternatives, and you pointed out rooftop uh dining and such, but uh to live along these fallacies that uh we must have outdoor dining uh impeding upon our streets, and that this is uh a lifeline.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: No, you're wrong.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: It's not so uh I think the way we're managing it right now from uh April until November is within reason, and uh I think you're off base with this.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: So one thing we don't need is between bike lanes and everything else, bus lanes, uh we don't need restaurants on our streets any longer.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And they become rat infested in disease.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: We don't need it, and a place for the homeless to sleep.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Um responding to your point about uh parking spaces, so at its peak, outdoor dining took less than one percent of our city's on-street parking spaces.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Um, and it's current iteration that has actually dropped to less than point one percent.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Um, so it's really not the majority takeover of parking spaces.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I disagree.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Totally.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: It those are those are numbers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Um, and then I will also just say this is a much better use of our public space than one individual's vehicle.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: This is the city of New York, Elizabeth.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: This isn't Denmark, this isn't Amsterdam.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Uh we live in a city with eight million people, and my husband drives a 52-foot tractor trailer.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Do you have any idea what it's like to navigate the streets of New York City when you're driving a 52-foot tractor trailer trying to make a living?

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Any idea?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: In the narrow streets of the getting around uh on the subway and walking um and using all of our public transportation?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: Um, it's important that we're thinking about how New Yorkers move around.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Elizabeth Adams
utterances.text: You're delusional and have space to do so.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Absolutely and totally delusional.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: But it doesn't surprise me coming from Transvault.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Michelle Cooper Smith, a Lower East Side resident and former community board member, testifies via Zoom that the permanent application process is too onerous and confusing for small business owners.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: So I just say this to make it clear that I am um very well versed in what's happening with the permanent outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, but I'm here to testify on my own um behalf, not on my community board's behalf.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um I'm a raving fan of outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um I still remember the July 4th weekend in 2020 when the emergency program started and how freeing it was to finally have a uh safe space to socialize with people after months of sadness and darkness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, and it gave me a vision of the future where we could have fewer private vehicles using our private curbs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, but I have to say that the new uh permanent process is far too onerous and frankly confusing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um I can't tell you how many business owners I've talked to in my neighborhood who like reasonably can't tell a difference between open streets and roadway um have uh roadway dining and silo cafes, and why are there different you know, month uh months that each operate in and whose approval for whom?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, and so I think that this should really be simplified.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, as others have said before, that roadway dining should be all year long, just like sidewalk cafe is like let's just make it much easier to understand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, and uh also I'll echo what was said earlier about how difficult the process is for um once they recognize the difference between the programs, how difficult the process actually is.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: CB notice, CB hearings where they get unnecessarily beat to death on hours and operations, DOT SLA, it's just all too much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Um, I agree with uh Councilmember O say that the admins seem to have designed the program to effectively kill it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: So it needs to be fixed by the council to be more straightforward for applicants and provide um more outdoor dining for New York City residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: And um, I think that the council should remove the provision that allows sidewalk cafes to be called up for a full council vote because as we all know, because of the zoning text amendment, all of these sidewalk cafes are now as of right, so long as they that DOTs are clean.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michelle Cooper Smith
utterances.text: Have a great day, thank you.

chapters.title: Samir Lavingia Testimony: Manhattan Community Board 5

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: I'm actually the transition environment vice chair, and I was on the open restaurants task force, and I'm currently on the committee that reviews all the outdoor dining applications at Manhattan Community Board 5.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: I love outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: It's so great to replace parking spaces with place for people to dine outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: And my quality of life has really improved significantly since being able to do so with my friends and family in my neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: I live near Columbus Circle that used to be so crowded on the sidewalks with the old sidewalk um requirements, and the new clear path requirements make it a lot easier to walk around to get around, especially in such a tourist-friendly area, and it's just such a better use of space in terms of what I think doesn't work and what could be fixed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: Right now, uh outdoor dining, uh you can only do it on Sundays after 10 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: I think it's a little bit silly that I can't have a breakfast on a Sunday morning uh before 10 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: outside.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: The process is very confusing for businesses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: We often see businesses have to have to hire expeditors uh and come to community board five, and I think the system should be a lot easier, so we need less of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: I think the program should be a year-round program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: Good weather isn't limited to certain times of the year anymore, and there are winter days where it's gorgeous outside, and um having to have restaurants break down their dining uh structures every time means that only Tony restaurants are able to open, which means affordable options for outdoor dining are really lacking in my neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: Uh so thank you for allowing me the time to speak, and I look forward to an improv improved version of the program in the future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Samir Lavingia
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Kathleen Collins, a lifetime New Yorker with a disability, testifies via Zoom in support of the Dining Out NYC program provided it is implemented correctly with proper ADA spacing and ramps.
chapters.title: Kathleen Collins Testimony: Disabled Resident Perspective

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: I'm disabled and I use a wheelchair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Um, I support the dining Out New York City program, provided it's done correctly and does not hamper or obstruct New Yorkers with disabilities' ability to participate in the dining Out New York City program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Since the dining out in New York City program provides another revenue source for restaurants by expanding their seating capacity, it also needs to be accessible to people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: People with disabilities enjoy outdoor dining with their family, relatives and friends.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Additionally, people with disabilities who want to hold a business meeting open me appreciate having outdoor dining as an option available to them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Thus, restaurants dining out New York City program needs to provide enough space between tables for people with mobility devices to be able to participate in the dining out New York City program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Sufficient tables that are low and can be accessed somewhere by someone sitting in a wheelchair needs to be provided.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Additionally, these tables need to provide leg space for people with wheelchairs to be able to come up to the table.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Further, the dining out New York City program needs to permit a customer to access it without a customer having to request a ramp or other assistance to be seated when other customers can independently seat themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Turning to the pedestrian walking past the dining out spaces, the restaurants need to keep a clear path on the sidewalk for everyone, including pedestrians with disabilities to travel safely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: Finally, in order to make sure restaurants outdoor dining actually is in compliance with regulations, the agency issuing these permits needs to be given financial resources to follow up and inspect the restaurants to make sure the restaurants are following the applicable safety, health, and accessible accessibility regulations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Kathleen Collins
utterances.text: In sum, the dining out New York City program can improve the quality of life of New Yorkers, provided it takes into consideration all the needs of everyone, including people with disabilities, and it is properly monitored.

summary: The Committees on Transportation and Infrastructure and Consumer and Worker Protection held a joint oversight hearing to evaluate the rollout of the permanent 'Dining Out NYC' outdoor dining program.

Small business owners and advocacy groups testified about significant challenges with the program, including high setup and storage costs due to seasonal roadway rules, confusing online forms, and a massive application backlog at the Department of Transportation.

The Department of Transportation defended its implementation, noting that over 2,600 restaurants are currently authorized to operate under conditional approvals, and committed to working with the Council to streamline the process.
title: Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure
